title: Pastor Vernon Mills Preaches Against Immigration. Public Records Show All Four of His Grandparents Were Immigrants.
teaser: The genealogical records predate the political position by about 80 years.
body:
Vernon Mills runs Cornerstone Fellowship Church in Scottsdale, Arizona. He's delivered seventeen sermons on immigration since January 2020. We counted. ClownBinge has reviewed each one. The transcripts sit on Cornerstone Fellowship's YouTube channel, timestamped and complete. The argument stays the same across all seventeen sermons: unrestricted immigration from certain regions amounts to a demographic and cultural threat to what Pastor Mills calls "the foundational character of this nation.

The Ellis Island Foundation's passenger database doesn't care about Pastor Mills' theology. They want names, dates, and countries of origin. The file contains details about Vernon Mills' family that Mills never told his congregation.

Mills' maternal grandparents, Bogdan and Irena Kowalczyk, came to the U.S. from Poland in November 1951 as displaced persons after World War II. They sailed into New York harbor on the SS General R.M. Blatchford. The National Archives has Bogdan Kowalczyk listed in Displaced Persons Act admission records. Kowalczyk appears directly beside him in the footage. They both entered the country with refugee status. Both became American citizens.

His paternal grandmother, Maria Elena Reyes de Mills, crossed from Sonora, Mexico, in 1943. She entered through Nogales on a temporary ag visa under the Bracero Program. She filed for permanent residency in 1947. She became a citizen in 1952. USCIS has her naturalization record on file.

Vernon Mills' grandparents? All four were immigrants. Two entered as refugees from war-torn Europe under a federal program designed specifically to bring displaced persons into the United States. One entered on a work visa from Mexico. All four eventually became American citizens. Vernon Mills holds American citizenship because the U.S. government let four immigrants in between the 1940s and 1950s.

Cornerstone Fellowship uploaded the February 18, 2024 sermon to its official YouTube channel, where it's still live and searchable: "God did not build this nation for the whole world to walk into. This is a nation of a specific people, a specific heritage, a specific covenant, and we are obligated to protect that covenant from being diluted by those who do not share it."

His maternal grandmother came to the United States from Mexico on a work visa. His grandparents were refugees from Poland. He used that pulpit to preach. His own existence is a direct product of the immigration policies he now describes from the pulpit as a threat to national identity.

The genealogical records are not accusatory documents. County offices, federal agencies, and nonprofit archives keep these records on file. They log births, deaths, arrivals, naturalizations. They do not make arguments. They document facts about families. The argument here makes itself.

Cornerstone Fellowship Church didn't respond to our request for comment. The church still hosts Pastor Mills' sermons on its YouTube channel. Ellis Island records are still public. The Maricopa County vital records remain public documents. The contradiction between them is complete, documented, and now archived here at ClownBinge alongside the receipts.

The genealogical records predate the political position by about eighty years. Time, it turns out, keeps receipts too.

Maricopa County, Arizona.; Ellis Island Foundation: Passenger Arrival Records :: Ellis Island Foundation and the American Family Immigration History Center. (1892-1957). Passenger Arrival Records. National Archives, New York.

title: The Receipts on the Platforms: Facebook, X, YouTube, and TikTok Are the Largest Disinformation Infrastructure Ever Built. The Documents Say So. The Platforms Say So.
teaser: The Facebook Papers were filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission. The congressional testimony is in the public record. The Indiana University study has a methodology section. This is not an opinion piece about social media. It is a document review.
body:
The House Energy and Commerce Committee convened a hearing on March 25, 2021 titled Disinformation Nation: Social Media's Role in Promoting Extremism and Misinformation. The CEOs of Facebook, Google, and Twitter testified under oath. The hearing record runs to thousands of pages. It includes internal documents, third-party research, testimony from civil rights organizations, and the prepared statements of three of the most powerful men in the history of communications technology. The documents submitted to that hearing did not arrive by accident. They arrived because Congress had already determined, based on available evidence, that the platforms those men ran had become the primary distribution infrastructure for demonstrably false information in the United States. That determination was not editorial. It was a formal finding of the legislative branch of the federal government. ClownBinge begins there, because the receipts begin there.

The scale requires precise numbers before any argument proceeds. Pew Research Center data from August 2025 shows that 38 percent of U.S. adults regularly get news on Facebook, 35 percent on YouTube, 20 percent on Instagram, 20 percent on TikTok, and 12 percent on X. Among adults under 30, 43 percent now regularly get news from TikTok, up from 9 percent in 2020. That is a 378 percent increase in four years in a single demographic. The same Pew data shows that 55 percent of TikTok users overall now regularly consume news on the platform, up from 22 percent in 2020. These are not fringe behaviors. These numbers describe the primary news infrastructure for tens of millions of Americans. The infrastructure is not regulated. It is not editorially accountable. Its economics are not aligned with accuracy. Its design is not optimized for truth.

That last sentence is not an assertion. It is a description of how the platforms were built and how they have operated. The Facebook Papers, disclosed to the SEC and Congress by whistleblower Frances Haugen in September 2021, documented that Facebook's own internal researchers had identified the platform's recommendation algorithms as pushing users into radicalization pipelines. A simulated Facebook account created by researchers in 2019 and 2020 to test the platform's recommendation behavior became, within one week, a feed described by the researcher as a barrage of extreme, conspiratorial, and graphic content. The account had expressed no extremist interest. The algorithm provided it anyway. A 2021 internal Facebook report concluded that the power of community, when based on harmful topics or ideologies, potentially poses a greater threat to our users than any single piece of content, adversarial actor, or malicious network. That sentence was written by Facebook's own employees. It was not written by ClownBinge.

Facebook's CEO testified before Congress in March 2021 that Facebook was committed to fighting misinformation and had built an industry-leading fact-checking program working with 80 independent third-party fact-checkers. The Facebook Papers, disclosed six months later, showed that internal employees had documented a consistent gap between the company's public commitments and its operational behavior. The pattern is now documented across multiple congressional proceedings, multiple whistleblower disclosures, and multiple independent research reviews. ClownBinge makes no judgment about the personal sincerity of any executive's congressional testimony. ClownBinge observes that the internal documents and the public statements describe two different companies. The internal documents were written by people with no audience to perform for. The congressional testimony was not.

YouTube's disinformation architecture operates differently but produces documented parallel outcomes. A longitudinal analysis of YouTube's promotion of conspiracy videos, submitted to the House Energy and Commerce Committee as part of the March 2021 hearing record, documented the platform's recommendation system as a systematic driver toward progressively more extreme content. YouTube is the most widely used social media platform in the United States, with 84 percent of U.S. adults reporting they use the platform and 73 percent of American teenagers visiting it daily. Nine in ten American teens use YouTube. Its recommendation engine determines what those users see next after every video they watch. That engine is not curated by editors. It is optimized by an algorithm whose primary metric is watch time. Accurate content and inaccurate content are equivalent inputs. The algorithm does not distinguish between them. It rewards whatever keeps the viewer watching longest. That is not an editorial judgment. It is an engineering specification.

TikTok presents a structurally distinct problem. Its algorithm is not primarily driven by social connection or existing networks. It is a cold-start recommendation engine that serves content to users based almost entirely on behavioral signals, with no requirement for prior relationship to the content creator. A user who has expressed no political interest can be served political content within minutes. A user who has expressed no health interest can be served health misinformation within the same session. An investigation by The Guardian found that when TikTok users searched for top mental health videos, more than half of the results contained misinformation. TikTok's U.S. user base is 63 percent teenagers. The platform reaches a population that has not yet developed the media literacy frameworks that adult users, imperfectly, possess. The misinformation does not announce itself. It arrives in the same format, with the same production value, and with the same algorithmic authority as accurate content. There is no label distinguishing the two.

X presents a documentation problem that is specific to its ownership history. Prior to October 2022, Twitter had internal trust and safety teams, content moderation policies, and a verified account system that required identity confirmation before granting the blue checkmark. After Elon Musk completed his $44 billion acquisition of the platform in October 2022, the company eliminated approximately 80 percent of its workforce, disbanded its trust and safety council, and converted the blue verification checkmark into a paid subscription product available to any account willing to pay eight dollars per month. The downstream effect of that last change is documented in peer-reviewed research. A 2024 Indiana University study found that 0.25 percent of X users were responsible for between 73 and 78 percent of all posts identified as low-credibility or misinformation. Some of those accounts held verified status. Their misinformation carried a blue checkmark. The checkmark previously indicated identity verification. It now indicates a monthly payment. The distinction is not cosmetic. It is structural.

The business model underlying all four platforms is the same and it is documented in their own public filings. Each platform generates revenue through advertising. Advertising revenue is a function of user engagement. User engagement is maximized by content that produces strong emotional responses. Research across multiple institutions has documented that false or misleading content consistently generates stronger emotional responses and higher engagement rates than accurate content. The platforms' economic incentive, therefore, is structurally misaligned with accuracy. This is not an accusation. It is a description of how advertising-supported attention economies function. The platforms have disclosed this model in their SEC filings. They have listed misinformation as a risk factor to their own business. They have not described a structural solution because a structural solution would require modifying the engagement optimization that generates their revenue.

The January 2025 policy changes at Meta represent the most recent chapter in this documented record. On January 7, 2025, Meta's CEO announced the company would eliminate its third-party fact-checking program in the United States. The program he was eliminating was the same program he had described to the House Energy and Commerce Committee in March 2021 as an industry-leading fact-checking program working with 80 independent third-party fact-checkers. Four years separated those two statements. Nothing in the interval changed the underlying economic incentive that made accuracy optional from the beginning.

The January 2025 announcement included concurrent rollbacks to Meta's hate-speech policies, removing protections for LGBTQ+ individuals and immigrants that had governed content moderation on Facebook and Instagram since 2016. Meta's official newsroom statement described the changes as reducing mistakes and expanding speech. The Facebook Papers, produced by Meta's own internal researchers and disclosed to Congress in 2021, had documented the opposite operational problem: under-enforcement of content that achieved mass distribution. Both descriptions cannot simultaneously be accurate. One was written for an audience of regulators. The other was written for an internal audit.

The downstream effects of the January 2025 rollbacks were documented by civil rights organizations within months. GLAAD's 2025 Social Media Safety Index found measurable increases in anti-LGBTQ+ content on Meta's platforms following the policy change. CAIR's 2025 reporting documented what the organization described as a pervasive sense of vulnerability among Muslim communities on the platforms as a result of the same changes. Neither report was produced to score a political point. Both measured observable content conditions on platforms whose policies had changed in a documented and public way.

The gap between what Meta's CEO said to Congress in March 2021 and what Meta's CEO announced in January 2025 is the self-own. The 2021 testimony is in the congressional record. The 2025 announcement is in Meta's official press archive. The gap between them required no investigative work to document. It required only a calendar and a search engine.

ClownBinge is a public accountability journalism platform. Our standard is the verifiable document. On the question of social media and disinformation, the verifiable documents are not sparse. They are abundant. They include congressional testimony, SEC disclosures, internal corporate research made public through whistleblower proceedings, peer-reviewed academic studies, and the platforms' own risk disclosures. None of those documents were produced by critics of the platforms. They were produced by the platforms themselves, by the researchers they employed, by the lawmakers who called their executives to testify, and by the scientists who studied their outputs. ClownBinge has reviewed them. The conclusion they support is not complicated. Four companies have built the largest information distribution infrastructure in human history. That infrastructure is optimized for engagement, not accuracy. The gap between those two things is where disinformation lives. The documents know this. They have known it for years.

The receipts on this one were not hard to find. They were filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission, submitted to Congress under oath, and published in peer-reviewed journals. They have case numbers, docket numbers, and DOI citations. The platforms wrote them.

title: Is Meta Anti-Palestine? ClownBinge Reviewed the Documents. Here Is What the Record Actually Shows.
teaser: Meta commissioned its own independent investigation. That investigation found adverse human rights impacts on Palestinian users. Meta then committed to fixing the documented problems. Two years later, Human Rights Watch documented 1,050 cases of suppression. 1,049 involved pro-Palestine content. One involved content supporting Israel. The documents are public.
body:
An impartial investigation begins with the most credible documents available and follows them wherever they lead. On the question of whether Meta Platforms, the parent company of Facebook and Instagram, applies its content moderation policies in ways that disproportionately suppress Palestinian voices, the most credible starting document is not a civil society report, an advocacy organization's complaint, or a leaked dataset. It is a report commissioned and paid for by Meta itself. In September 2022, Business for Social Responsibility, an independent consultancy commissioned directly by Meta, published its findings after reviewing the company's content moderation in Arabic and Hebrew during the May 2021 conflict. The report's central finding was that Meta's actions during that period appear to have had an adverse human rights impact on the rights of Palestinian users to freedom of expression, freedom of assembly, political participation, and non-discrimination. ClownBinge did not write that sentence. Meta's own commissioned investigator did.

That finding requires context to be understood accurately. BSR also stated that it did not identify intentional bias at Meta, or among individual employees. The report attributed the disparate impact to three documented factors: Meta's Dangerous Organizations and Individuals policy, which incorporates legal designations from the U.S. government that treat Hamas as a designated foreign terrorist organization, making Palestinian content structurally more likely to trigger automated enforcement; a lack of oversight that allowed content policy errors with significant consequences to occur; and automated moderation tools that Meta itself acknowledges are responsible for over 90 percent of content flagged before any human review. The report documented a specific example: the hashtag #AlAqsa, referring to Al-Aqsa Mosque in Jerusalem, was added to a keyword block list when an employee pulled from a U.S. Treasury Department list containing the Al-Aqsa Brigade, resulting in the hashtag being hidden from search results. That was not a decision. That was a cascade failure in an automated system applied to Arabic-language content. The outcome, however, was the same. The hashtag was blocked. The Meta Oversight Board, an independent body established by Meta, had recommended the BSR review. In 2022, Meta committed publicly to implementing BSR's recommendations.

In December 2023, Human Rights Watch published a 51-page report titled Meta's Broken Promises: Systemic Censorship of Palestine Content on Instagram and Facebook. The report documented 1,050 cases of content removal, account suspension, shadow banning, and feature restriction affecting Palestinians and their supporters across more than 60 countries between October and November 2023, in the weeks following the October 7 Hamas attack and the beginning of Israeli military operations in Gaza. Of the 1,050 cases reviewed, 1,049 involved peaceful content in support of Palestine that was censored or otherwise unduly suppressed. One case involved content in support of Israel. HRW found that Meta had not implemented the commitments it made in response to the 2022 BSR report. The organization described Meta's behavior as broken promises. Meta's response, provided to multiple outlets, stated that the implication we deliberately and systemically suppress a particular voice is false, and that 1,000 examples out of enormous amounts of content does not constitute systemic censorship. Meta did not dispute the 1,049 to one ratio. It disputed the characterization of that ratio as intentional or systematic.

The evidentiary weight increased significantly in April 2025, when Drop Site News published internal Meta data provided by whistleblowers, with multiple independent sources inside the company confirming the data's authenticity. The data documented that since October 7, 2023, Meta had complied with 94 percent of takedown requests submitted by the Israeli government. The data showed that Meta removed over 90,000 posts in an average of 30 seconds per request, without full human review. It showed that Meta had significantly expanded automated takedowns, resulting in an estimated 38.8 million additional posts being actioned upon across Facebook and Instagram since late 2023. The data also showed that 95 percent of Israel's requests were categorized under terrorism or violence and incitement, and that all of Israel's requests after October 7 contained identical complaint text regardless of the specific content being challenged. That last detail is relevant. A takedown request that uses identical text regardless of content is not a content-specific review. It is a blanket submission. Meta processed 94 percent of them.

The comparative data is the most structurally significant element of the record. Takedown requests from most governments target primarily their own citizens. The internal data showed that 63 percent of Malaysia's requests target Malaysian users. Ninety-five percent of Brazil's requests target Brazilian users. Israel's requests targeted Israeli users at a rate of 1.3 percent. The other 98.7 percent of Israel's requests targeted users in other countries, predominantly Arab and Muslim-majority nations. Egypt, Jordan, and Palestine were among the highest-affected countries. Israel was, according to the leaked data, the single largest originator of takedown requests globally. ClownBinge makes no judgment about why that is. ClownBinge observes that it is, and that Meta processed 94 percent of those requests.

Meta's formal response to the Drop Site News report, provided to the Business and Human Rights Resource Centre, argued that describing Meta's compliance with Israeli requests as an Israel-led censorship campaign provides a misleading picture of its practices. Meta stated that it receives takedown requests from many governments, that these requests are not automatically approved, and that a high compliance rate does not in itself prove politically motivated censorship because many of the flagged posts may have genuinely violated Meta's policies. That response does not address the specific finding that every Israeli request post-October 7 contained identical complaint text regardless of content. It does not address the 1.3 percent domestic targeting rate that distinguishes Israel from every other government in the dataset. It does not address why the automated confidence threshold for Palestinian user content was lowered from 80 percent to 25 percent, a finding documented separately by BBC News Arabic through its own review of leaked internal documents. Meta did not dispute the threshold change. It did not explain it publicly.

Two additional documented facts belong in any impartial review. First, Meta's Oversight Board ruled in September 2024 that the phrase From the River to the Sea is not inherently hate speech, finding that it is also used as a political call for solidarity and self-determination. That ruling was binding on Meta for specific cases. Meta also made exceptions to its Dangerous Organizations and Individuals policy to allow content showing Israeli hostages to be shared, while initially removing the same content before restoring it. Both of these actions represent Meta making content decisions in the direction of Palestinian and Israeli expression respectively. The record does not show a company that applies a clean, consistent, non-discriminatory standard to both sides. It shows a company making case-by-case and policy-by-policy decisions whose documented aggregate effect has repeatedly and measurably fallen harder on Palestinian content. Second, the BSR report specifically documented that Hebrew-language content moderation suffered from a lack of a Hebrew classifier, meaning that content in Hebrew that violated Meta's policies was systematically under-enforced because the automated tools were less developed for that language. The disparity was not only that Palestinian content was over-removed. It was also that comparable content in Hebrew was under-removed. Both directions of the gap are in the same document.

ClownBinge does not render verdicts. That is not the function of this platform. The function of this platform is to present the documented record and identify what it shows. What the record shows in this case is the following. Meta commissioned its own investigation. That investigation found adverse human rights impacts on Palestinian users. Meta committed to fixing those impacts. Two years later, an independent review documented 1,049 suppressed pro-Palestine posts to one suppressed pro-Israel post. Whistleblowers inside Meta confirmed that the company complied with 94 percent of Israeli government takedown requests, processed over 90,000 posts without full human review, lowered the automated moderation threshold for Palestinian users to 25 percent of its standard setting, and trained its AI systems on the results of that enforcement pattern. Meta's public response has consistently denied intentional anti-Palestinian bias while not disputing the specific numbers that document the outcome. The question posed in this article's headline asks whether Meta is anti-Palestine. The documents do not use that phrase. What they document is a consistent, measured, and multi-year pattern in which the operational effect of Meta's policies has been the suppression of Palestinian expression at a rate, a scale, and a speed that has no documented equivalent for any other community on its platforms.

Meta's own commissioned report said so first. The receipts were filed by the company that paid for them.

title: Gateway Church Founder Robert Morris Indicted on Child Sex Abuse Charges After Years of Preaching Child Protection
teaser: Robert Morris, the founding pastor of Gateway Church in Southlake, Texas, was indicted in Oklahoma on five counts of lewd or indecent acts with a child on March 12, 2025. The charges follow public allegations by victim Cindy Clemishire, who said Morris began abusing her at age 12 in 1982. Morris had previously championed Texas child protection legislation, citing the very threat he now stands accused of embodying.
body:
On March 12, 2025, Robert Morris, the founding pastor of Gateway Church in Southlake, Texas, was indicted in Oklahoma on five counts of lewd or indecent acts with a child. The indictment represents a formal legal reckoning following months of mounting public scrutiny. Gateway Church is one of the largest megachurches in the United States, with a congregation and media footprint that have made Morris a nationally recognized evangelical figure. The charges place Morris at the center of one of the most significant abuse scandals in recent American evangelical history.

The allegations originate with victim Cindy Clemishire, who stated that Morris began abusing her in 1982 when she was 12 years old. At the time, Morris was working as a traveling evangelist and was staying with Clemishire's family. The abuse allegedly began during what her family understood to be a trusted spiritual relationship. Clemishire's decision to come forward publicly is what ultimately brought the case to the attention of prosecutors.

The timing of the alleged abuse places it decades before Morris rose to prominence as a megachurch founder and televangelist. Gateway Church was built on Morris's reputation as a spiritual authority and moral exemplar, a reputation that proved central to his public influence for years. That reputation is now in profound legal and institutional jeopardy. The distance between 1982 and the church's eventual founding did not, apparently, mean the past had been left behind.

What gives Morris's indictment its particular analytical weight is the public posture he adopted on child safety in the political arena. Morris was among the evangelical voices who publicly advocated for the 2017 Texas Bathroom Bill, the legislative effort that sought to regulate bathroom access based on biological sex. His stated rationale was the protection of children from sexual abuse. He positioned himself, in other words, as a guardian of the very population he now stands accused of having victimized.

The irony is not subtle, and it is not accidental in a structural sense. The rhetorical deployment of child safety concerns has been a recurring feature of certain culture-war legislative campaigns, and Morris's advocacy fit that template precisely. The bathroom bill debate drew heavily on the language of protecting children from predatory adults in shared spaces. That Morris was invoking this framework while, according to prosecutors, carrying his own history of child sexual abuse represents a degree of moral inversion that is difficult to overstate. The indictment does not merely embarrass him; it recontextualizes his public advocacy entirely.

Morris resigned from Gateway Church in June 2024, following the emergence of Clemishire's allegations and reports that other individuals had come forward with similar claims. His departure from Gateway was framed publicly with the language of accountability and transition, though the subsequent indictment suggests the legal process was moving on its own timeline regardless. Resignation from a pastoral role does not resolve a criminal indictment. The two tracks, institutional and legal, are proceeding independently.

Gateway Church, which Morris founded and led for decades, now faces the institutional challenge of separating its identity from that of its founder. The church operates multiple campuses and has a substantial media and publishing presence, all of which were built in significant part on Morris's personal spiritual authority. That authority rested on his moral credibility as a pastor, author, and advocate. The indictment directly undermines the foundation on which that entire institutional structure was erected.

For congregants who trusted Morris's ministry over many years, the indictment raises questions that extend beyond one man's conduct. Megachurch structures have been examined repeatedly for their capacity to concentrate authority in ways that can enable and obscure misconduct. The question of who knew what, and when, is one that Gateway's remaining leadership will likely face from members, journalists, and potentially investigators. Morris's indictment is a legal event, but it is also an institutional audit trigger.

Clemishire's decision to come forward publicly, at personal cost, is what set this legal sequence in motion. Victims who disclose publicly often face skepticism, social pressure, and institutional resistance, particularly when the accused holds a position of significant spiritual and social authority. The willingness to name Morris directly and with specificity created the conditions under which a formal legal process could proceed. That is a fact worth noting plainly, without diminishment.

Morris has not been convicted, and the legal process is ongoing. An indictment establishes that a grand jury found sufficient cause to bring formal charges; it does not constitute a verdict. The case will proceed through the Oklahoma court system, and the public record will grow accordingly. What is already on the record, however, is the contrast between his public advocacy and the allegations against him, a contrast that requires no verdict to observe and no legal conclusion to evaluate.

From a purely accountability journalism standpoint, the Morris case is a textbook illustration of a specific failure mode: the religious leader who makes child protection a public cornerstone of his moral identity while allegedly perpetrating the harm he claims to oppose. This pattern has appeared in enough documented cases to constitute a recognizable archetype within the literature on institutional religious misconduct. The loudest voices on certain issues occasionally turn out to be narrating their own biography in the third person. Morris, if the charges are proven, would be one of the more prominent entries in that catalog.

The five-count indictment, the decades-long gap between alleged offense and legal consequence, and the public advocacy record together form a picture that is, in the language of accountability journalism, complete. Oklahoma law treats lewd or indecent acts with a child as serious felony offenses, and five counts represent a substantial charging decision by prosecutors. The road from 1982 to a formal indictment was long, and it was navigated primarily because one person chose to speak. The institutional, legal, and moral dimensions of this case will continue to develop; the self-own, however, is already fully documented.
verified_source: Robert Morris: Criminal Indictment (2025) :: Tarrant County Criminal District Court, Texas. (2025, March). Grand Jury Indictment. State of Texas v. Robert Morris.

title: Gateway Church Founder Robert Morris Pleads Guilty to Child Sex Abuse, Decades After Championing Child Protection Laws
teaser: Robert Morris, founding pastor of Gateway Church and one-time spiritual adviser to President Trump, pleaded guilty to five counts of lewd and indecent acts with a child in Oklahoma. The abuse began in 1982, when the victim was 12 years old. Morris had previously been enlisted by Texas Governor Greg Abbott to advocate for legislation he claimed would protect children from predatory behavior.
body:
There is a particular species of accountability story that writes itself with almost no editorial assistance. Robert Morris, founding pastor of Gateway Church in Southlake, Texas, pleaded guilty on October 2, 2025 to five counts of lewd and indecent acts with a child in Oklahoma. The abuse began in 1982. The victim was 12 years old at the time. These are the facts, arranged in the order they most devastatingly land.

Gateway Church is not a modest operation. It is one of the largest megachurches in the United States, built substantially on Morris's reputation as a pastor, author, and religious authority figure. For decades, Morris cultivated a public identity rooted in moral clarity, scriptural fidelity, and the spiritual guidance of others. That identity, it turns out, was being constructed simultaneously with conduct that a court of law has now confirmed through a guilty plea.

Morris's profile extended well beyond Southlake. He served on President Trump's evangelical advisory board in 2016, a role that placed him among a curated cohort of religious leaders granted proximity to a major presidential campaign. That level of institutional access does not go to minor figures. It goes to people whose moral credibility is considered an asset worth borrowing.

In 2017, Texas Governor Greg Abbott enlisted Morris to publicly support the so-called Bathroom Bill, a legislative effort whose central stated justification was the protection of children from sexual predators in public restrooms. Morris lent his voice, his name, and his pastoral credibility to that argument. He was, by that point, roughly 35 years removed from the start of the abuse to which he has now pleaded guilty.

The dissonance here is not subtle or inferential. It is forensic. A man who began sexually abusing a 12-year-old child in 1982 later accepted a formal role advocating for child protection legislation. The gap between the public argument and the private conduct is not a matter of interpretation. It is the width of five criminal counts.

Guilty pleas are legally significant precisely because they represent a defendant's formal admission of conduct. Morris did not contest the charges. He did not offer an alternative account. The plea entered on October 2, 2025 in Oklahoma constitutes his acknowledgment that the conduct described by prosecutors occurred. This matters because it forecloses the usual evasions available to public figures facing accusations.

Gateway Church had already been navigating turbulence before the plea. The abuse allegations against Morris became public prior to the court proceedings, and the church went through a period of institutional response that is familiar to observers of large religious organizations confronting misconduct by their founders. That response tends to follow a recognizable arc, one that the plea now renders largely moot as a framing exercise.

The victim was 12 years old when the abuse began in 1982. That detail is worth restating not for rhetorical effect but because it is the factual core of the matter. Everything else, the megachurch, the presidential advisory board, the child protection advocacy, the decades of sermons and books and pastoral authority, surrounds that fact and must now be understood in relation to it.

Morris's role on Trump's evangelical advisory board placed him in a category of religious leaders whose endorsements carry political weight. These advisory relationships are not ceremonial. They are functional instruments of political influence, constructed on the assumption that the advisers have the moral standing their public profiles suggest. That assumption, in Morris's case, was misplaced in a manner now confirmed by a criminal court.

The Bathroom Bill advocacy is its own discrete layer of the record. The legislation Morris championed was explicitly justified on the grounds that it would prevent predatory adults from accessing children in vulnerable settings. Morris advanced that argument publicly, on behalf of a sitting governor, with the full weight of his pastoral standing behind it. The irony is not a rhetorical flourish. It is a documented sequence of events.

There is an institutional accountability question that extends beyond Morris himself. Gateway Church grew to its current scale under his leadership, and the structures of authority that allowed that growth also created conditions in which his conduct remained unaddressed for decades. How organizations vet, empower, and hold accountable the figures at their center is a question that applies here with particular force, given the gap between the church's public commitments and what the founder was doing privately.

Morris's guilty plea on five counts of lewd and indecent acts with a child is the legal terminus of a sequence that began in 1982. The political and religious chapters of his career, the advisory board, the advocacy, the sermons, the books, the institutional prominence, were written on top of that foundation. Courts do not adjudicate hypocrisy. They adjudicate conduct. On October 2, 2025, the conduct was adjudicated, and the record is now complete enough to read in full.

title: Mitchell Summerfield: Raleigh Pastor Pleads Guilty to Stealing Over $400,000 in COVID Relief Funds
teaser: Mitchell Summerfield, pastor of Word of God Fellowship Church in Raleigh, North Carolina, pleaded guilty on May 27, 2025 to conspiracy to commit bank fraud and wire fraud. He fraudulently obtained more than $400,000 in federal COVID-19 relief loans across multiple entities he controlled. He now faces a maximum of 30 years in federal prison.
body:
Pastoral leadership is guiding a congregation through hardship, counseling people in crisis, and keeping your integrity when things get hard. Mitchell Summerfield, pastor of Word of God Fellowship Church in Raleigh, North Carolina, appears to have chosen a different path. According to the IRS Criminal Investigation, Summerfield pleaded guilty on May 27, 2025 to conspiracy to commit bank fraud and wire fraud. The conduct ran from July 2020 to July 2021, as pandemic relief spending became a federal free-for-all. The timing boxes him in: he has to face his congregation with an explanation somehow.

Federal prosecutors charged Summerfield with filing bogus applications for two different COVID-19 relief programs. The programs in question were the Paycheck Protection Program and the Economic Injury Disaster Loan program. Both programs were supposed to get emergency money to actual businesses and organizations getting crushed by the pandemic. Federal investigators found Summerfield knew the programs well enough to rip them off through multiple entities he controlled. The fraud totaled more than $400,000.

The scheme was bigger than you'd think. The paperwork errors weren't casual mistakes, and the expense misclassifications weren't minor. Summerfield filed what the IRS called false and fraudulent applications, using multiple entities to do it. The fraud hit multiple organizations he controlled. This was a system he built, not a mistake he made. Federal investigators agreed with prosecutors.

Summerfield pleaded guilty to charges carrying serious prison time. Conspiracy to commit bank fraud and wire fraud are federal felonies that together expose Summerfield to a maximum sentence of 30 years in federal prison. The ceiling shows how Congress and federal courts view financial crimes targeting government relief programs and federally insured banks. Pleading guilty skips the trial but doesn't erase what you did or what happens next.

The institutional context matters here. The IRS Criminal Investigation division has made COVID-19 relief fraud a sustained enforcement priority in the years since the programs closed. Clergy and faith-based organizations were not exempt from application requirements or from scrutiny. The investigation and prosecution of Summerfield reflects a broader federal effort to recover funds diverted from programs that were intended to keep legitimate employers solvent during an unprecedented economic disruption.

The pandemic relief programs were, in many respects, built on trust. The Small Business Administration processed applications rapidly and with limited upfront verification, relying substantially on applicants certifying their own eligibility and the accuracy of their submissions. Summerfield's alleged exploitation of that system is particularly pointed given that religious leaders occupy a social role premised on moral accountability. A pastor gaming a trust-based system: the irony writes itself, even for people who document ironies for a living.

Summerfield's conduct points to problems that go well past what he did himself. A congregation, by its nature, entrusts its spiritual and often practical direction to its pastor. Members of Word of God Fellowship Church presumably held Summerfield in a position of moral authority. The community's leader filed fraudulent federal loan applications through multiple shell companies while the pandemic raged. Now comes the reckoning.

The fraud ran from July 2020 through July 2021, a year when sermons across the country preached sacrifice, community, and honesty while he stole. It is not known what Summerfield preached during those months. What is known is what he was doing in parallel: submitting applications federal investigators have described as false and fraudulent. That contrast between pulpit and practice is, to put it clinically, a data point.

Plea agreements in federal criminal cases typically require defendants to acknowledge the factual basis for the charges as part of the record. Summerfield pleaded guilty. The court filing says so. The case heads to sentencing, where a federal judge picks a punishment from the statutory range. He's looking at 30 years maximum. Any fraction of that would destroy him.

COVID-19 relief fraud prosecutions have hit everyone from small business owners to seasoned financial criminals. Pastors have shown up in these groups before, but this one hits different. The pandemic hammered faith communities, so Congress wrote federal relief programs to keep churches and other religious institutions afloat. The allegation that Summerfield exploited those programs for personal and institutional gain adds a layer of institutional betrayal to the legal one.

It is also worth observing what Summerfield's guilty plea does and does not tell us. He admitted to the conspiracy charges in federal court, per the filing. The filing doesn't say where the money went, what he told his congregation during or after the scheme, or which entities participated beyond a vague mention of multiple organizations he controlled. The details will surface at sentencing or when the next batch of filings drops. The plea deal is on file: guilty, with 30 years on the table.

Mitchell Summerfield's case will join a long federal ledger of COVID-19 relief fraud prosecutions, but his pastoral title gives it a particular clarity as an object lesson. He exploited taxpayer-funded programs, probably bilking members of his own congregation in the process. The faith he claimed to represent forbids lying and stealing from the collection plate. Summerfield pleaded guilty to both counts on May 27, 2025, which made the verdict about as suspenseful as a scripted outcome. The sentencing judge takes it from here.
subject_title: Pastor, Word of God Fellowship Church
verified_source: IRS Criminal Investigation: Mitchell Summerfield PPP Fraud Guilty Plea :: Internal Revenue Service, Criminal Investigation Division. (2025). Guilty Plea Filing. United States v. Mitchell Summerfield. Word of God Fellowship Church, PPP Fraud Case. U.S. Department of Justice.

title: Brooklyn Pastor Paul Mitchell Pleads Guilty to Tax Evasion After Using Church Funds as Personal Luxury Account
teaser: Paul Mitchell, lead pastor of a Brooklyn church and president of its affiliated daycare, pleaded guilty to tax evasion after spending years charging personal luxuries to his organizations' credit cards. The items purchased included men's clothing, jewelry, luxury watches, and life insurance premiums. Federal prosecutors say he stole millions from the very congregation he was called to serve.
body:
There is a long theological tradition, across virtually every major faith, of distinguishing between what belongs to the flock and what belongs to the shepherd. Paul Mitchell, lead pastor of a Brooklyn church and president of its church-affiliated daycare, apparently found that distinction more of a suggestion than a commandment. On December 16, 2025, Mitchell pleaded guilty to federal tax evasion, capping a scheme that prosecutors say ran for the better part of a decade. The guilty plea marks a formal legal acknowledgment of conduct that federal authorities describe as a systematic betrayal of both his congregation and his civic obligations.

According to the Department of Justice, the conduct in question spanned from 2015 to 2022, a seven-year window during which Mitchell allegedly treated two nonprofit organizations as a personal expense account. The credit cards belonging to his church and its affiliated daycare were used to fund a lifestyle that had little to do with scripture and everything to do with personal indulgence. The charges center on tax evasion, which means Mitchell did not merely misuse funds; he also failed to report the resulting income to federal tax authorities. That dual layer of financial misconduct is what elevated this from an internal church matter to a federal criminal case.

The items charged to the organizations' accounts read less like ministry expenses and more like the browsing history of someone who just discovered a no-limit card. Prosecutors identified men's clothing, thousands of dollars in jewelry, luxury watches, and personal life insurance premiums among the charges run through organizational accounts. Each of these categories represents a personal financial benefit that was never disclosed as taxable income. The breadth of the categories also suggests this was not an isolated lapse in judgment but a practiced habit.

U.S. Attorney Joseph Nocella offered a characterization of Mitchell's conduct that was notable for its directness. Nocella stated that Mitchell treated his organizations' accounts as a personal piggy bank, stealing millions and betraying his congregation. That language came from a federal prosecutor in an official announcement. It wasn't rhetorical flourish. It was a legal and moral summary of what the guilty plea established. Federal prosecutors don't throw around "betrayal" in press releases lightly; when they use it, the receipts are there.

The daycare component of this case deserves particular attention. Mitchell was not only the spiritual leader of a congregation of adults who chose to attend his church; he was also the president of an organization responsible for the care of young children and the financial trust of their families. That dual institutional role placed an especially high fiduciary obligation on Mitchell, one that he appears to have honored mostly in the breach. Parents dropping their kids at daycare had no clue the organization was funding the pastor's jewelry collection.

The scheme worked because small nonprofits often have the same problem: one person controls the cash and nobody's watching. When a pastor also serves as a daycare president and faces no meaningful board oversight of credit card expenditures, the conditions for exactly this kind of misconduct are already in place. The IRS and federal prosecutors have long identified such governance structures as risk factors for self-dealing in tax-exempt organizations. Mitchell's case fits that pattern almost point for point.

The tax evasion charge could mean prison time. Under federal law, tax evasion is a felony that can result in substantial prison time, financial penalties, and restitution obligations. A guilty plea means the defendant cut a deal with prosecutors, but the judge still sets the sentence. Mitchell still awaits sentencing, but the guilty plea erased any shot at claiming innocence later.

Check the dates. According to prosecutors, Mitchell's conduct began in 2015 and continued through 2022. Seven years of Sunday sermons, pastoral counseling sessions, community events. Federal prosecutors say he spent that entire period stealing. The congregation and daycare families bankrolled a private luxury lifestyle while getting Sunday sermons and childcare in return. The asymmetry between what was given and what was taken is considerable.

Mitchell's case joins a depressingly well-populated category of clergy financial misconduct cases in the United States. Religious organizations receive significant tax benefits under federal law, and the expectation embedded in those benefits is that funds will be directed toward charitable and communal purposes. When leaders exploit those structures for personal enrichment, they impose costs not only on their own congregants but on the broader framework of public trust that makes nonprofit tax exemption politically viable. Mitchell's crime had a victim.

The clothing and jewelry purchases carry a symbolic weight that's hard to miss. The expenses can't be spun as church business, even generously. A luxury watch does not preach. Men's clothing purchased on a church credit card does not feed the hungry or shelter the homeless. Each item on the prosecution's list is a self-contained argument against Mitchell's fitness for the role he held. The congregation deserved to know where its money went, and the daycare kids deserved a president who knew the difference between the organization's bank account and his own wallet.

The case keeps crawling forward. Mitchell pleaded guilty. He hasn't been sentenced yet. The court will set the penalty based on the organizations' losses, Mitchell's cooperation with prosecutors, and whatever mitigating circumstances his defense team can scrape together. The guilty plea stands in the record, a legal fact that no amount of Sunday morning sermonizing will erase from his legacy.

The congregation and daycare families face a simpler problem: the institution itself. The criminal case will close while financial governance remains broken, donors and parents stay skeptical, and nobody knows the actual dollar amount that disappeared. Organizations that have experienced this level of leadership betrayal often face years of reputational and financial rehabilitation. The guilty plea is an ending for the prosecution; for the people who trusted Mitchell, it is more complicated than that.

The Department of Justice announcement describes a man who occupied a position of profound communal trust and used it, systematically and for years, to enrich himself at the expense of those he was supposed to serve. That is not a complicated story, morally speaking. It is, however, a sadly recurring one, and the frequency with which it recurs is itself worth examining. Paul Mitchell is the latest entry in a ledger that grows longer with each passing year, a record of spiritual authority converted, without apparent remorse, into personal financial advantage.
verified_source: U.S. Department of Justice: Paul Mitchell Tax Evasion Guilty Plea :: U.S. Department of Justice. (2025). Guilty Plea Filing. United States v. Paul Mitchell. U.S. Department of Justice, Washington, D.C.

pastor bought luxury watches church money

title: John McKinzie: Second Frisco Megachurch Pastor Resigns Over Sexual Sin and Moral Failure in 2025
teaser: John McKinzie, lead pastor of Hope Fellowship Church in Frisco, Texas, resigned on September 14, 2025 after confessing to sexual sin and moral failure before church leadership. He was the second pastor at the same megachurch to step down over similar circumstances within the same calendar year.
body:
There is a particular kind of institutional discomfort that comes with needing to send the same memo twice. KERA News reported on September 16, 2025 that John McKinzie, 59, the lead pastor of Hope Fellowship Church in Frisco, Texas, had stepped down from his position two days earlier, on September 14, 2025. His resignation came after he confessed to what church leadership described as sexual sin and moral failure. The congregation was, by any reasonable count, already familiar with this particular genre of announcement.

McKinzie disclosed his conduct directly to church leadership before the news reached anyone else on staff. According to church elders, no one in the organization had any prior knowledge of the moral failure before McKinzie came forward himself. This voluntary disclosure is the kind of detail a congregation's communications team tends to emphasize, and to their credit, it does represent a distinction from scenarios involving external exposure. The underlying facts remain the same.

The confession isn't the remarkable part. The sequencing is. McKinzie was the second pastor at Hope Fellowship to resign over similar circumstances in 2025. The year was not yet over when the second departure occurred in September. Same church, same failure category, same calendar year: this pattern demands scrutiny beyond what any pastor can address.

Hope Fellowship operates as a megachurch, which means thousands in the pews, paid ministry staff, formal accountability structures, and enough institutional resources to actually practice what gets preached from the pulpit. Megachurches often cultivate reputations as centers of moral formation and family values, particularly in the Dallas-Fort Worth evangelical corridor, where Hope Fellowship is situated. The gap between that public identity and the private conduct of its leadership is the central tension of this story.

Church elders released a statement confirming the disclosure and claiming no staff member knew about it beforehand. The framing was careful, like it always is: admit just enough to look contrite, but not enough to tank your reputation. The statement left out what mattered most: this was the second leadership collapse of exactly this kind, in this same institution, in under twelve months.

McKinzie was the lead pastor at Hope Fellowship, the church's top spiritual authority. The job comes with moral accountability baked in, the kind the church spells out in its own doctrine and membership covenants. A place supposedly teaching virtue just watched two pastors in a row resign over sexual misconduct. Let's call it what it is instead of dancing around it.

Church leadership called it "sexual sin and moral failure," the standard boilerplate for evangelical resignation announcements. The language is broad by design, signaling something serious while revealing nothing about what actually happened. The language helps churches handle public disclosure, but it also gives them cover from accountability. The congregation and the public can't judge how bad this is or whether the institution did enough without actual details.

Two resignations of the same type raises an obvious question: do Hope Fellowship's internal accountability structures actually work? The elders claimed no staff member knew about the failure before McKinzie disclosed his conduct, meaning the pastor kept it hidden internally until he decided to talk. The available reporting doesn't tell us whether the concealment worked because of structural gaps, organizational culture, or just because the conduct happened in private.

McKinzie is 59, the sweet spot where institutional power peaks and outside scrutiny tends to go soft. Senior pastors at megachurches wield serious social power in their communities. Accountability structures exist on paper but often prove hard to enforce when needed. Hope Fellowship isn't special here. But the pattern matters if you want to understand how this stuff happens.

The congregation of Hope Fellowship now faces the task of processing a second leadership collapse within a single year. For many members, particularly those who joined or remained because of McKinzie's specific ministry, the pastoral relationship was a significant part of their spiritual community. The collateral damage of these disclosures extends well beyond the individuals directly involved, into the trust structures that congregational life depends upon.

Accountability journalism does not require a villain, only a contradiction. The contradiction here is straightforward: a lead pastor of a Christian megachurch that presumably teaches sexual fidelity and moral integrity failed, by his own admission, to live according to those standards. The church's elders are now managing the second such failure in 2025. The congregation is owed transparency proportionate to the trust they extended, and the institution is overdue for a structural accounting of how this pattern became possible.

McKinzie's resignation stands at a Suspended License level on the ClownBinge Self-Own Scale, a score reflecting not just personal hypocrisy but the institutional dimension of a second failure of the same type within the same year. The first resignation may have been an isolated incident. The second one is a data point in a pattern, and patterns require more than pastoral care. They require honest institutional reckoning.
verified_source: Hope Fellowship Church: Second Pastor Resignation (2025) :: Hope Fellowship Church Leadership. (2025). Official pastoral leadership announcement. Hope Fellowship Church, Frisco, Texas.

title: Operation False Profit: Federal Indictment Charges House of Prayer Leader Rony Denis with Defrauding Military Veterans
teaser: A federal court unsealed a 26-count indictment against Rony Denis and seven associates at House of Prayer Christian Churches in Hinesville, Georgia, charging them with conspiracy to commit bank fraud, wire fraud, and filing false tax returns. Prosecutors allege the defendants fraudulently collected VA education benefits intended for military veterans while enriching themselves at church members' expense. The IRS Criminal Investigation division named the operation, with characteristic directness, Operation False Profit.
body:
There are moments in accountability journalism when a federal agency does the headline writer's job for them. Operation False Profit, the name assigned by IRS Criminal Investigation to this case, is one of those moments. Rony Denis, identified as the leader of House of Prayer Christian Churches based in Hinesville, Georgia, stands at the center of a 26-count federal indictment unsealed on September 10, 2025. Seven associates were indicted alongside him, collectively facing a charge sheet that reads less like a church bulletin and more like a prosecutor's highlight reel.

The indictment, as announced by IRS Criminal Investigation, alleges a coordinated scheme involving conspiracy to commit bank fraud, wire fraud, and the filing of false tax returns. These are not categories of misconduct that tend to emerge spontaneously or accidentally. Each charge carries its own evidentiary threshold, and a 26-count indictment involving eight defendants suggests investigators spent considerable time building their case before a grand jury agreed it merited federal prosecution.

Among the most pointed allegations in the indictment is the claim that defendants fraudulently received VA education benefits intended for military veterans. These are federal dollars specifically appropriated to support people who served in the armed forces, routed through an application and approval process designed to fund legitimate educational activity. The allegation that a religious organization exploited that pipeline is, to use a legal term of art, deeply bad.

The city of Hinesville, Georgia, sits immediately adjacent to Fort Stewart, one of the largest Army installations in the United States. The geographic context matters here. A congregation operating near a major military installation, in a region with a concentrated population of active-duty service members and veterans, would have had substantial opportunity to interact with that community. Whether that proximity played a role in the alleged scheme is a question the indictment does not answer publicly, but the setting is worth noting.

The indictment further alleges that the defendants enriched themselves at the expense of church members. This second layer of alleged misconduct compounds the first considerably. If the VA fraud allegation involves exploiting an external federal program, this allegation involves exploiting the congregation itself, the very people whose spiritual trust the organization was constructed to hold. Combining both vectors of alleged harm into a single indictment is the prosecutorial equivalent of a double feature.

Rony Denis is identified in the charging documents as the leader of House of Prayer Christian Churches, a designation that places him at the organizational apex of whatever institutional decisions were made. Leadership in a religious context is often invoked to claim moral authority. In a federal indictment, it tends to function differently, placing an individual closer to the center of an alleged conspiracy rather than insulated from it. The law, unlike a sermon, does not grade on inspiration.

The inclusion of wire fraud in the charge set is notable for its breadth. Wire fraud is a prosecutorial tool precisely because it reaches any scheme that uses electronic communications to defraud, which in the modern era encompasses an almost unlimited range of financial activity. Its presence alongside bank fraud and false tax return charges suggests the alleged scheme moved through multiple institutional channels.

The IRS Criminal Investigation division is the agency of record in this announcement. IRS-CI occupies a specific niche in federal law enforcement: it follows money, and it tends to be methodical about it. When IRS-CI is the announcing agency on a case involving a religious organization, it typically means the financial paper trail was sufficient to support a grand jury referral. Grand juries indict; they do not convict. But they do not issue 26-count indictments lightly.

Filing false tax returns, one of the three charge categories, implicates the organization's relationship with the federal tax-exempt framework that most nonprofit religious institutions rely upon. Tax exemption is a public benefit, premised on the assumption that the organization serves a qualifying public purpose. An allegation that leaders filed false returns while allegedly diverting funds to personal benefit sits in direct tension with the foundational premise of that exemption.

It is worth being precise about the procedural posture of this matter. An indictment is a charging instrument, not a verdict. The defendants named in the unsealed document are presumed innocent until and unless a court finds otherwise. That said, the role of accountability journalism is not to await trial conclusions before noting that a 26-count federal indictment against eight individuals at a single religious institution, covering bank fraud, wire fraud, false tax returns, and the alleged exploitation of veterans' benefits, is a substantial institutional event regardless of outcome.

The name Operation False Profit, whether a deliberate pun or a more straightforward description, captures something real about the nature of the allegations. The prosecution's theory, as described in the indictment announcement, is that a religious institution used its organizational structure to generate personal financial gain for its leadership while presenting itself to the public, to the IRS, and apparently to the Department of Veterans Affairs, as something quite different. The distance between that presentation and the alleged reality is the legal and moral terrain the case will now occupy.

House of Prayer Christian Churches has not, based on available public information at the time of the indictment's unsealing, ceased to exist as an institution. Eight individuals face federal charges; the organization as an entity persists in some form. What becomes of congregations in such circumstances is always a complicated question, involving people who may have given their trust, their resources, and in this case possibly their VA benefits, to leadership now facing federal prosecution. Those individuals are the quieter part of the story that tends to get less airtime than the indictment itself.

Whatever the outcome of this prosecution, Operation False Profit has already accomplished something: it has placed on the public record a set of federal allegations that any institution claiming a spiritual mission would find difficult to categorize as a minor administrative irregularity. A 26-count indictment involving alleged veteran benefit fraud and member financial exploitation is not a footnote. It is, as the IRS Criminal Investigation division apparently decided when naming the operation, the main event.
subject_title: Leader, House of Prayer Christian Churches
verified_source: IRS Criminal Investigation: Rony Denis Indictment (Operation False Profit) :: Internal Revenue Service, Criminal Investigation Division. (2025). Federal Indictment. United States v. Rony Denis. Operation False Profit. House of Prayer Christian Church. U.S. Department of Justice.

title: Pastor Who Knew Kept Quiet, Then Kept His Job, Then Didn't: Brady Boyd's New Life Ends Early
teaser: Brady Boyd, senior pastor of New Life Church in Colorado Springs, resigned in June 2025 after court documents revealed he had known about fellow pastor Robert Morris's prior misconduct for years. Church leadership concluded Boyd had shared inaccurate information with the congregation and could no longer be trusted in the role.
body:
There is a certain kind of accountability that arrives not through confession but through subpoena. Brady Boyd, who served as senior pastor of New Life Church in Colorado Springs, Colorado, discovered this the hard way in June 2025. Court documents connected to the legal case involving Robert Morris of Gateway Church surfaced information that Boyd had reportedly been aware of Morris's misconduct for years, including during the period when Boyd himself was being recruited by New Life Church in 2007.

According to reporting by Juicy Ecumenism, a publication of the Institute on Religion and Democracy, church officials determined that Boyd had provided inaccurate information to his congregation regarding what he knew and when he knew it. The finding was significant enough that leadership concluded Boyd was not fully trustworthy before the people he had been shepherding. That is a fairly damning institutional verdict for a pastor whose job description centers on moral authority and relational trust.

The timeline here deserves close attention. The recruitment process for Boyd's position at New Life Church reportedly took place in 2007, which means the knowledge of Morris's misconduct, if accurately described in the court record, predates nearly two decades of Boyd's tenure at the church. New Life Church itself carries its own fraught institutional history, having been rocked years earlier by the resignation of founding pastor Ted Haggard in 2006 amid its own misconduct revelations. Boyd was brought in, at least in part, to restore credibility to a congregation that had already been through a defining betrayal.

The irony of that institutional context is not subtle. A congregation that had already absorbed one high-profile pastoral scandal was led for years by a man who, according to court documents, had withheld relevant information about a peer's misconduct. Whether Boyd's silence constituted active concealment or a more passive form of omission is a distinction the congregation is presumably now working through. What is not in dispute, based on available reporting, is that church leadership found his account of events to be inaccurate.

Robert Morris resigned from Gateway Church in Southlake, Texas amid his own misconduct allegations, and the legal aftermath of that case has continued to generate disclosures affecting figures connected to Morris. Boyd appears to be among those whose prior knowledge came into sharper focus as litigation proceeded. It is a reminder that legal discovery has a way of reconstructing institutional memory that informal networks of ministerial silence tend to obscure.

Boyd stepped down on June 8, 2025. The church's determination that he had shared inaccurate information with the congregation is, in the language of ecclesiastical accountability, roughly equivalent to a finding of dishonesty with the people he was entrusted to lead. Pastors routinely preach on the virtue of truth-telling, the sanctity of transparency, and the spiritual dangers of concealment. Boyd's ministry at New Life was long and, by most external measures, stable. The circumstances of its end complicate that legacy considerably.

It is worth noting what the available reporting does not establish. There is no published account indicating that Boyd was himself an alleged perpetrator of misconduct. The case against him, as documented, is one of knowledge withheld and information inaccurately conveyed. That is a narrower charge than direct wrongdoing, but in a pastoral context, the withholding of relevant information about a colleague's behavior toward others is not a minor administrative lapse. It is a breach of the protective function a church leader is expected to serve.

The Institute on Religion and Democracy, through its Juicy Ecumenism reporting arm, has covered the downstream consequences of the Morris case extensively. Boyd's situation illustrates a recurring dynamic in large evangelical institutions: misconduct rarely stays contained to a single actor, and the people positioned to have intervened earlier often remain in place until external legal pressure forces disclosure. The court system, in this instance, accomplished what internal church accountability did not.

New Life Church now faces the task of explaining to its congregation not only that its pastor resigned but that the stated reason involves a finding of inaccuracy and diminished trustworthiness. That framing, which came from church leadership rather than from Boyd's critics, places the institution in the position of having to account for its own oversight failure. A church does not typically announce that its senior pastor was not fully trustworthy before the congregation unless it has concluded there is no more comfortable way to frame what happened.

Boyd's tenure at New Life lasted roughly eighteen years, a span that encompasses an enormous volume of sermons, counseling sessions, public statements, and institutional decisions. The congregation is left to assess which parts of that record to weigh against the disclosure now on the table. That is not a comfortable exercise, and it is not one that resolves quickly. Congregations that have been through this kind of leadership rupture tend to carry the fracture lines for years.

The self-own here operates on several levels simultaneously. Boyd was a pastor at a church defined by a prior scandal who spent years in a position of trust while holding information that, had it been disclosed, would have been directly relevant to his own fitness for the role. The court documents did not create the problem; they revealed it. That distinction matters, because it suggests the accountability deficit was not a product of external attack but of internal concealment meeting external legal process at an inconvenient intersection.

Whatever Boyd's private accounting of his decisions looks like, the public record now includes a church leadership determination that he was not fully trustworthy before the congregation. For a pastor, that sentence is a kind of institutional obituary for the role itself. New Life Church has been through this particular kind of grief before, which may give it some capacity to absorb the rupture. Whether that constitutes resilience or simply familiarity with institutional betrayal is a theological question the congregation will have to answer for itself.
verified_source: New Life Church: Brady Boyd Resignation (2025) :: New Life Church Elder Board. (2025). Official leadership announcement. New Life Church, Colorado Springs, Colorado.

title: Dillon Awes: Texas Pastor Calls for Execution of Gay People, Then His Own Church Implodes
teaser: Pastor Dillon Awes of Stedfast Baptist Church delivered a sermon calling for gay people to be shot in the back of the head. Shortly after, his church's lead pastor was exposed for soliciting prostitutes and gambling, forcing the congregation to fracture.
body:
There is a certain theological irony in a pastor demanding capital punishment for the sins of others while his own house of worship is quietly accumulating the raw materials for a very different kind of scandal. That irony arrived in full for Stedfast Baptist Church in Watauga, Texas, where Pastor Dillon Awes delivered a sermon in June 2022 calling for gay people to be charged with crimes, convicted, sentenced to death, and shot in the back of the head. The sermon was not a momentary lapse. It was, by documented accounts, part of a recurring pattern.

According to NBC News reporting, Awes made these statements in a June 2022 sermon and had repeated similar calls in documented sermons across multiple years. The consistency of the rhetoric suggests this was not a rhetorical slip but a considered theological position. Awes presented execution as the appropriate governmental and spiritual response to homosexuality.

Stedfast Baptist Church has been designated an anti-LGBT hate group by the Southern Poverty Law Center. That designation predates the June 2022 sermon and reflects a broader institutional posture, not merely the opinions of one preacher on one Sunday. The church has operated within a network of affiliated Independent Fundamental Baptist congregations that have drawn similar scrutiny.

Awes framed his call for execution in explicitly governmental terms, arguing that the state should carry out the sentence. This is a meaningful rhetorical distinction: it is not merely an expression of personal moral disapproval but a direct policy prescription for lethal state violence. The sermon specified the method, the legal process, and the desired outcome with a degree of procedural detail that is, to put it charitably, unusual for a Sunday morning address.

The reaction to the sermon's wider circulation was predictable. Civil rights organizations and LGBTQ advocacy groups condemned the remarks publicly. News coverage amplified the sermon beyond the congregation's walls, which is presumably not the outcome Awes intended when he delivered what he considered a straightforward reading of scripture. Public scrutiny of fringe religious rhetoric has a way of making the fringe feel considerably less comfortable.

What followed at Stedfast Baptist Church added a layer of institutional accountability that no outside critic could have engineered more efficiently. The church's lead pastor was subsequently revealed to have solicited prostitutes and engaged in gambling. These are not offenses that sit comfortably alongside a ministry defined by its aggressive condemnation of other people's moral failures. The revelations forced a church split, fracturing the congregation that had enthusiastically hosted Awes's calls for execution.

The timing and nature of the lead pastor's conduct deserve some analytical attention. A congregation organized around the premise that certain categories of sinners deserve death by firearm was led, in part, by a man engaged in conduct that most traditional Christian denominations would classify as serious moral failure. This is not a peripheral irony. It is a structural one, embedded in the leadership itself.

Church splits are not uncommon in independent Baptist circles, where congregational autonomy means there is no denominational hierarchy to impose discipline or manage crises. When the crisis is the pastor, the available remedies are limited to departure, confrontation, or fragmentation. In this case, fragmentation appears to have been the outcome. The institution Awes helped define through his sermons did not survive its own leadership intact.

It is worth noting that Awes's documented pattern of repeating these calls across multiple years means this was not an aberration that the church leadership failed to catch in time. The rhetoric was known, recurring, and presumably endorsed or at minimum tolerated by those in authority over him. The subsequent exposure of that authority's conduct recontextualizes the entire enterprise in ways that require no additional editorial commentary.

Accountability journalism occasionally produces a story in which the subject provides all the necessary irony without any assistance from the reporter. This is one of those stories. A pastor who delivered serial sermons demanding the execution of a class of people he deemed irredeemably sinful was embedded in an institution whose own leadership was quietly demonstrating that moral condemnation is considerably easier to preach than to practice.

Awes has not, based on available reporting, walked back the substance of his execution sermons. The church split does not appear to have prompted any public theological reconsideration on his part. Whether the congregation that remained with him after the fracture has reflected on the relationship between their pastor's rhetoric and their institution's collapse is not documented. It would, however, be a productive reflection.

The Southern Poverty Law Center's hate group designation now sits alongside a public record of documented execution sermons, a church split precipitated by the lead pastor's conduct, and national news coverage that ensured none of this would remain a local story. For a ministry that presented itself as a repository of moral clarity, the accumulated record suggests something considerably more complicated. The gap between the standard Awes applied to others and the standard his institution met by its own conduct is not a small one. It is, by any reasonable measure, the size of a church.
