The acquittal is the system partially correcting itself, imperfectly, expensively, and at enormous cost to defendants who have already experienced arrest, pretrial detention, and the full weight of a prosecution. It is not evidence that the defendants were not engaged in any conduct. It is evidence that the prosecution overreached.

What the Wrongful Conviction Record Says About the Reliability of Conviction Data

The National Registry of Exonerations, maintained by the University of Michigan Law School and Michigan State University College of Law, tracks every known post-conviction exoneration in the United States since 1989. The findings do not require interpretation. Black people are 7 times more likely to be wrongfully convicted of murder than white people. Black people represent approximately 53% of all homicide exonerations while comprising roughly 13% of the U.S. population. The average Black exoneree serves 3.9 more years in prison before exoneration than white exonerees. In cases involving sexual assault where the victim was white, Black defendants represent 50% of exonerations.

The Innocence Project has documented that cross-racial eyewitness misidentification is the leading contributing factor in wrongful convictions, and that cross-racial identification error rates are substantially higher than same-race identification rates across both laboratory studies and real-world case analysis. The conviction record, which is foundational to the argument that Black Americans commit more crime, contains a documented and quantified error rate that is itself racially asymmetric. The record is not a neutral tally. It is the output of a system whose failure patterns have been measured, and those failure patterns fall disproportionately on Black defendants.

The Empirical Record, In Full

The claim that FBI arrest statistics prove Black Americans commit more violent crime fails at the level of basic methodology, before it reaches the level of social science or policy. Arrest data measures policing intensity and deployment decisions, not crime commission. The drug arrest data proves this with precision unavailable in most policy debates: the same behavior, across racial groups, documented by a separate federal health agency, produces a 3.73-to-1 arrest disparity. That disparity cannot be explained by behavioral difference. The federal health survey eliminates that explanation directly.

When crime is measured by surveying victims directly, bypassing police deployment entirely, the racial disparities change substantially and track poverty concentration rather than race. When sociologists control for structural disadvantage, the racial disparity in crime largely disappears from the statistical model. When the wrongful conviction record is examined, Black defendants are exonerated at rates that document systemic failure in the conviction process. And the crime data architecture is structurally blind to the categories of crime most associated with white institutional actors, whose aggregate economic harm to American workers and investors exceeds street crime by every measure available.

Historically and through the present, anti-Black political actors have cited one data source, misread what that source measures, ignored the corrective literature, and never asked what the primary sources outside the arrest record say. The Bureau of Justice Statistics, Stanford University, Harvard University, the University of Michigan Law School, the ACLU, SAMHSA, and the Vera Institute have all asked. Their answers are consistent with one another and inconsistent with the narrative. This article has assembled those answers in one place. The citations are below. Read them before repeating the claim.
verified_source: Bureau of Justice Statistics: National Crime Data :: Bureau of Justice Statistics. (2024). National Crime Victimization Survey and Crime Statistics. U.S. Department of Justice, Washington, D.C.; Stanford Open Policing Project: Traffic Stop Data :: Stanford Open Policing Project. (2024). Research on Racial Disparities in Policing. Stanford University, Stanford, California.; National Registry of Exonerations: Exoneration Records :: National Registry of Exonerations. (2024). Exoneration Records Database. University of Michigan Law School and Michigan State University College of Law.

title: Harvest Spoiled: James MacDonald Fired After Recordings, Fraud Allegations, and a Hunting Ranch on the Lord's Dime
teaser: James MacDonald built Harvest Bible Chapel into a congregation of 13,000 before his elders fired him in February 2019 after recordings captured him making vulgar statements about colleagues and a journalist. Subsequent reporting documented alleged insurance fraud, personal misuse of church funds, and years of spiritual abuse.
body:
There is a particular theological irony in a pastor who preaches accountability being held accountable by his own elders, his own recordings, and eventually his own congregation. James MacDonald, founder of Harvest Bible Chapel in Rolling Meadows, Illinois, arrived at exactly that irony on February 12, 2019. The elders of the church he built fired him. The instrument of his dismissal was, in part, audio he apparently did not know was being recorded.

MacDonald had cultivated a significant national profile as an evangelical leader, author, and radio broadcaster over the course of his tenure at Harvest. The church he founded expanded to roughly 13,000 members, a scale that places it firmly in the category of American megachurch. Influence at that scale requires a corresponding degree of institutional trust, which makes what followed particularly instructive. As documented by the Chicago Tribune, the elders' decision was not made in a vacuum; it came after recordings surfaced that called MacDonald's character and conduct into serious question.

The recordings in question captured MacDonald making vulgar statements about colleagues and about a journalist who had been investigating him. The specific content of those statements was reported as sufficiently damaging that the elders determined continued pastoral leadership was untenable. This is a notable sequence of events: a man whose professional identity rested on moral authority was undone, at least in part, by his own unguarded words about people scrutinizing his unguarded conduct. The symmetry is not subtle.

The journalist element of this story deserves particular attention. Investigative reporters do not typically appear in pastoral termination letters by accident. Someone had been asking questions about Harvest Bible Chapel before the recordings surfaced, which suggests the institutional problems were not entirely invisible to outside observers prior to the firing. MacDonald's apparent hostility toward that scrutiny, captured on recording, likely accelerated a reckoning that was already developing. Newsrooms and church elders are not natural allies, but they occasionally arrive at the same conclusion through different doors.

Beyond the recordings, subsequent investigations surfaced a more detailed picture of alleged financial misconduct. MacDonald was reported to have pressured church staff to make fraudulent insurance claims, a category of behavior that sits well outside any reasonable interpretation of pastoral duty. The mechanism here, pressuring subordinates rather than committing acts directly, is a recognizable feature of institutional misconduct and carries its own set of moral and legal implications.

Church funds were also allegedly directed toward personal expenses, including costs associated with a hunting ranch. The hunting ranch detail has a specific texture to it. It is not merely the scale of the alleged misappropriation that registers; it is the specificity. A hunting ranch is a personal luxury item, not an organizational necessity, which makes the reported co-mingling of funds difficult to characterize as administrative confusion. It reads as a deliberate blurring of the line between institutional resources and personal benefit.

The financial allegations did not exist in isolation. Investigators and former church members also documented a sustained pattern of bullying and spiritual abuse spanning years under MacDonald's leadership. Spiritual abuse as a category describes the misuse of religious authority to manipulate, demean, or control individuals within a faith community. It is a well-documented phenomenon in high-control religious environments, and its presence alongside financial misconduct is consistent with broader patterns observed in megachurch scandal literature.

What emerges from the documented record is not a single dramatic failure but an interlocking set of institutional pathologies. Financial misuse, pressure on subordinates to commit potential fraud, verbal contempt for colleagues and journalists, and a multi-year pattern of abusive behavior are not independent data points. They describe an organization structured in ways that allowed one person's conduct to go unchecked for an extended period. The elders who fired MacDonald were, in most cases, the same institutional structure that had previously enabled his authority.

Harvest Bible Chapel's growth to 13,000 members is relevant context rather than an incidental detail. Large congregations generate substantial financial resources, and they concentrate social and institutional authority in leadership figures in ways that smaller faith communities typically do not. The reporting on MacDonald's alleged conduct suggests that this concentration of resources and authority was exploited rather than stewarded. That dynamic is not unique to Harvest, but it is particularly visible here.

MacDonald's public ministry had been built significantly on themes of biblical fidelity, personal integrity, and strong leadership. His radio program, Walk in the Word, extended his reach well beyond the Rolling Meadows campus. A public platform predicated on moral instruction does not automatically make its host a moral exemplar, but it does raise the stakes considerably when the gap between instruction and conduct becomes visible. The gap documented here was not narrow.

The February 12, 2019 firing was not an endpoint so much as a threshold. The recordings prompted the termination; the subsequent investigations filled in the institutional picture that the recordings had only sketched. Church institutions are often reluctant to publicize internal misconduct, which means that what becomes publicly documented typically represents a portion of what occurred. The documented record here is already substantial, which suggests the full picture is not lighter.

Accountability journalism exists precisely for situations in which institutional self-interest would otherwise allow documented misconduct to remain unexamined. The Chicago Tribune's reporting on Harvest Bible Chapel represents that function operating as designed, and the outcome, a firing, a congregation left to reckon with years of alleged abuse, and a prominent pastor's public ministry in ruins, is the kind of result that makes the work legible. MacDonald built an institution around the idea that leaders are accountable to higher standards. His elders and a journalist took that premise seriously. He apparently did not expect them to.
subject_title: Founder and Former Senior Pastor, Harvest Bible Chapel
verified_source: Harvest Bible Chapel: James MacDonald Termination Statement (February 2019) :: Harvest Bible Chapel Elder Board. (2019, February 12). Official Elder Board Statement: James MacDonald. Harvest Bible Chapel, Rolling Meadows, Illinois.

title: Bishop Eddie Long, Crusader Against Same-Sex Marriage, Faces Lawsuits Alleging Sexual Coercion of Teenage Boys
teaser: Bishop Eddie Long built a national profile as a defender of traditional marriage and a vocal opponent of same-sex relationships. In September 2010, four young men filed lawsuits alleging he had used his spiritual authority and material gifts to coerce them into sexual relationships that began when they were teenagers. The gap between the pulpit and the allegations was not subtle.
body:
Eddie Long was, by any measure, a significant figure in American evangelical Christianity. As senior pastor of New Birth Missionary Baptist Church in Lithonia, Georgia, he presided over a congregation of roughly 25,000 members, a number that placed his church firmly in the megachurch tier of American religious life. His influence extended well beyond Sunday services, shaping local politics, community organizing, and the national conversation around faith and family values. That influence is precisely what makes the events of September 2010 worth examining with some care.

On September 22, 2010, four young men filed lawsuits against Long, as reported by the New York Times. The lawsuits alleged that Long had used his position as a spiritual authority, combined with gifts and material benefits, to coerce the young men into sexual relationships. According to the reporting, the alleged conduct began when the men were teenagers. The lawsuits did not arrive quietly; they landed in a media environment already primed to notice the distance between the accusations and the man's public record.

That public record was considerable, and specifically relevant. Long had been a leading organizer of a march against same-sex marriage in Atlanta in 2004. The march was not a casual gesture; it was a deliberate, high-profile act of civic and religious advocacy positioning Long as a defender of what he and others called traditional marriage. His activism on the issue was well documented and formed a central part of his national religious identity. He was not merely a pastor who held conventional views; he was someone who organized communities around those views.

The lawsuits alleged a specific mechanism of coercion worth understanding. The young men did not describe a relationship of equals. They described a dynamic in which Long's spiritual authority, his role as a mentor and father figure, and his capacity to provide gifts and opportunities were all used instrumentally to establish and maintain sexual contact. This framing places the allegations squarely within a recognized pattern of institutional religious abuse, where the authority of the office becomes the mechanism of harm. It is a pattern that accountability institutions, legal systems, and journalists have documented repeatedly across denominations.

Long had cultivated a specific kind of relationship with young men in his congregation through a program that brought teenagers and young adults into close mentorship under his guidance. That program was, by all public accounts, presented as a form of spiritual formation and community investment. The plaintiffs alleged it provided something else as well: access. Access is the precondition for the kind of conduct described in the lawsuits, and understanding how it was structured matters to any serious reading of the situation.

The congregation Long led was not a small community gathering in a rented hall. Twenty-five thousand members represents a substantial institutional footprint. At that scale, a pastor is less a spiritual guide and more an institutional leader, with staff, budgets, legal counsel, and communications infrastructure. The gap between that institutional weight and the individuals who filed the lawsuits, who came to Long as teenagers seeking mentorship, is not incidental to the story. It is the story.

It is worth being precise about what was filed and what was not, as of the date of the incident. The lawsuits were civil complaints, not criminal charges. The legal standards differ, and the outcomes in civil litigation against public religious figures have historically varied considerably. The distinction between civil and criminal proceedings is real and legally meaningful. Reporting accurately on allegations requires acknowledging that filing a lawsuit is not equivalent to a court finding of liability. What is not in dispute is that four individuals made serious formal legal claims on a specific date.

Long's public response in the days following the filing was closely watched by religious media and the broader press. His congregation, by virtue of its size and his public prominence, was itself a kind of public institution. How a congregation of 25,000 processes allegations against its founding pastor is itself a matter of public interest, not merely internal church business. The institutional response to credible allegations of misconduct by a senior leader is one of the more reliable indicators of whether accountability is genuine or performative.

The specific combination of elements in this case is worth naming plainly. A pastor who publicly organized opposition to same-sex marriage and relationships stood accused of using his pastoral authority to coerce young men, who had come to him as teenagers, into sexual contact. The contradiction is not simply personal; it is structural. Long did not merely hold private views that conflicted with private behavior. He organized public civic action around those views, lending them the force of community mobilization and moral leadership.

That structural dimension is what distinguishes this incident from ordinary personal failure. A private person who holds views inconsistent with their behavior is a study in human complexity. A public religious leader who uses institutional authority to advocate against a category of relationship, and who then stands accused of using that same institutional authority to engage in alleged conduct with teenagers, has created a specific kind of public accountability problem. Institutional authority, in this reading, is not background; it is the instrument.

For the congregation itself, the September 2010 filings represented an acute crisis. Members who had organized their spiritual and in many cases social lives around Long's leadership faced an immediate and difficult question about the relationship between their faith community and the person who led it. The impact on individual congregants tends to receive less attention than the institutional or celebrity dimensions of such cases, but it is arguably the most consequential human element. Twenty-five thousand people had a stake in what happened next.

Long's story became a significant chapter in the broader ongoing reckoning with clergy misconduct and institutional religious accountability in American life. The pattern of a prominent anti-gay religious figure facing allegations of same-sex conduct was not new in 2010, and the specific addition of alleged coercion of teenagers placed it in an even more serious category of concern. Scholars and journalists who track these patterns note that the public advocacy itself, the marches, the sermons, the political organizing, often functions as a kind of credibility shield that makes the eventual allegations more devastating when they emerge.

What the September 2010 lawsuits established, without adjudication, was a factual predicate for public accountability: four individuals made formal legal claims alleging serious misconduct by a man who had used his public platform to advocate, loudly and organizationally, for a particular vision of sexual morality. That predicate is the appropriate starting point for accountability journalism, which does not require a verdict to identify a meaningful public contradiction. The distance between the 2004 march and the 2010 lawsuits is measurable in years; the distance between the public persona and the allegations is harder to quantify, but it is not small.
subject_title: Senior Pastor, New Birth Missionary Baptist Church
verified_source: Fulton County Superior Court: Eddie Long Sexual Misconduct Civil Suits (2010-2011) :: Fulton County Superior Court, Georgia. (2010-2011). Civil Complaints: Spencer LeGrande v. Bishop Eddie Long et al.; Jamal Parris v. Bishop Eddie Long et al.
tags: Bishop Eddie Long same-sex marriage hypocrisy

title: Ted Haggard: God's Loudest Anti-Gay Voice Was Paying a Male Escort for Three Years
teaser: Ted Haggard, president of the National Association of Evangelicals and a leading opponent of gay marriage, resigned in November 2006 after a male escort alleged Haggard had been his client for three years. The escort also alleged Haggard had purchased methamphetamine from him.
body:
There is a particular kind of institutional irony that arrives not with a whisper but with a full press release and a letter of resignation. That is precisely what arrived on November 2, 2006, when Ted Haggard, one of the most recognizable evangelical voices in the United States, stepped down from the organization he had helped define. The circumstances were not ambiguous, and they were not going away quietly. A male escort had gone to the press, and the press had obliged.

Haggard had served as president of the National Association of Evangelicals, an organization representing approximately 30 million American Christians. That is not a niche constituency. That is a theological constituency roughly the size of California, with opinions on policy, morality, and the divine order of marriage. Haggard had been their designated spokesman, and he had performed the role with considerable conviction.

The man who came forward was Mike Jones, a Denver-based male escort who alleged that Haggard had been his paying client for approximately three years. Jones did not describe a single lapse in judgment. He described a three-year professional arrangement. The allegations also included the claim that Haggard had purchased methamphetamine from Jones, which added a second and distinctly secular layer of contradiction to the story.

To appreciate the full geometry of this situation, one must consider where Haggard was standing when it collapsed. He had been a prominent and vocal opponent of same-sex marriage, lending his considerable platform and his organization's institutional weight to efforts to restrict marriage to heterosexual couples. His theological objections were not peripheral. They were central to his public identity.

Beyond the pulpit, Haggard had cultivated a relationship with the executive branch of the federal government that most pastors do not achieve and most lobbyists would envy. He reportedly met weekly with President George W. Bush and served as a regular faith adviser to the Bush White House. His influence was not merely congregational. It was federal.

The timing of the disclosure was worth noting. Jones came forward just days before the November 7, 2006 midterm elections, at a moment when same-sex marriage ballot measures were appearing across multiple states and evangelical voter mobilization was considered a critical component of Republican electoral strategy. The revelation landed at the intersection of theology, politics, and logistics.

New Life Church, the Colorado Springs congregation Haggard had founded, was also swept into the fallout. Haggard stepped down from leadership there as well, recognizing, or being advised, that the gap between his public position and the allegations now in print was too wide to bridge from the pulpit. The church, which had grown under his leadership into a significant evangelical institution, was left to process a founder-level credibility crisis.

The National Association of Evangelicals moved with notable speed to separate the organization from its now-former president. Institutional self-preservation operates on a faster timeline than theological deliberation, and the board did not take weeks to reach a conclusion. Haggard's resignation was accepted, and the process of organizational distancing began with the kind of efficiency that suggests the mechanism had been prepared in advance, at least as a contingency.

What makes the Haggard case analytically distinct from a routine scandal of personal failing is the scaffolding of policy and advocacy that surrounded it. A pastor who preaches fidelity and has an affair has committed a recognizable and tragically common contradiction. A pastor who spends years lobbying against the legal recognition of same-sex relationships while allegedly maintaining a paid relationship with a man for three years is operating in a different register of institutional contradiction entirely.

The policy influence Haggard wielded was not incidental to this story. He had not merely expressed personal religious beliefs from a private pulpit. He had carried those beliefs into the West Wing, into federal policy conversations, and into the public square as the representative voice of 30 million constituents who presumably believed he meant what he said.

Methamphetamine, for its part, does not appear anywhere in the evangelical policy platform as a recommended recreational substance. Its alleged presence in this narrative functions less as an addendum and more as a kind of editorial underlining. The story did not require additional complication, and yet complication arrived anyway, which is perhaps the most reliable characteristic of situations built on sustained concealment.

Accountability journalism has a professional obligation to resist the cheap satisfaction of the obvious punchline. This story provides so many that restraint becomes its own discipline. What remains after the restraint is applied is a factual record of a man who held institutional authority over the moral direction of millions of Americans, who met weekly with a sitting president to discuss values and policy, and who was simultaneously, according to his accuser, doing something else entirely on a regular basis for three years. The record stands without editorial embellishment.
subject_title: Former President, National Association of Evangelicals; Founder, New Life Church
verified_source: National Association of Evangelicals: Haggard Resignation Statement (November 2006) :: National Association of Evangelicals Board of Directors. (2006, November 3). Official Statement: Ted Haggard resignation as NAE President. National Association of Evangelicals, Washington, D.C.; New Life Church: Haggard Pastoral Termination (November 2006) :: New Life Church Restoration Team. (2006, November). Official Statement: Ted Haggard terminated as Senior Pastor. New Life Church, Colorado Springs, Colorado.

title: Tony Spell: Louisiana Pastor Defies Government Authority, Then Cashes Government Check
teaser: Tony Spell rallied over 1,000 congregants in defiance of Louisiana's pandemic emergency order, declaring the government had no authority over his church. He then applied for and accepted a federal Paycheck Protection Program loan from that same government.
body:
There is a certain theological clarity required to simultaneously declare the government spiritually illegitimate and financially useful. Tony Spell, lead pastor of Life Tabernacle Church in Central, Louisiana, appears to have located that clarity with remarkable speed in the spring of 2020. His journey from constitutional defiance to federal loan recipient took a matter of weeks and required no apparent period of reflection.

On March 31, 2020, Spell held in-person church services attended by more than 1,000 people. This was a direct violation of Louisiana Governor John Bel Edwards' emergency stay-at-home order, which had been issued in response to the rapidly escalating COVID-19 pandemic. Louisiana was, at that moment, one of the hardest-hit states in the country. Spell held the services anyway.

According to the Associated Press, Spell was subsequently charged with six counts of unlawful assembly. These were not administrative technicalities or bureaucratic misunderstandings. These were criminal charges arising from a documented, deliberate decision to gather a congregation of over a thousand people during a public health emergency. Spell did not express regret. He expressed a theological framework.

That framework centered on the assertion that the government possessed no legitimate authority over the church. This is a position with a long history in certain strands of American religious thought, and it is not, in isolation, an indefensible one. Churches have historically and appropriately pushed back against government overreach. The specific application here, however, involved rejecting public health orders designed to slow the spread of a virus that was actively killing people in Louisiana at the time.

Spell leaned into his stated convictions publicly and forcefully. He framed the services as an act of faith and resistance, positioning his congregation as standing firm against secular governmental intrusion into sacred space. The imagery was clear: the church answers to a higher authority, and that authority does not issue emergency declarations from the governor's office. It was a compelling rhetorical posture, assuming one never intended to apply for a federal loan program.

The Paycheck Protection Program, created under the federal CARES Act, was administered by the United States Small Business Administration, an agency of the very federal government whose earthly authority Spell had publicly dismissed. The program was, by design, a lifeline offered by governmental structures to organizations willing to accept governmental assistance under governmental terms. Spell requested and accepted that lifeline. The theological nuances of this transition were not publicly explained.

It is worth pausing on the sequencing. First, Spell declared governmental authority spiritually illegitimate. Then he gathered over a thousand people in violation of a governmental emergency order. Then he was charged with six criminal counts under governmental law. Then he accepted money from the federal government. This is not a story about a pastor who struggled privately with a complex tension between church autonomy and civic obligation. This is a story about a man who found the government's authority entirely convincing when it came with a check attached.

The PPP loan acceptance is not merely ironic in a superficial sense. It represents a meaningful structural contradiction. If a government has no authority over a religious institution, it also has no particular standing to offer that institution material support. Accepting the support implicitly acknowledges the relationship that Spell's public rhetoric had categorically rejected. You cannot simultaneously be outside a system and a beneficiary of it, at least not without acknowledging the inconsistency.

Louisiana's legal proceedings against Spell continued into the period after the March 31 services. The six counts of unlawful assembly represented a serious legal exposure. Spell's defense, consistent with his public framing, rested significantly on religious liberty arguments. Those arguments have genuine legal weight in American jurisprudence, and courts have historically taken First Amendment religious liberty claims seriously. They do not, however, resolve the PPP contradiction.

Life Tabernacle Church is a Pentecostal congregation, a tradition that places significant emphasis on the direct movement of the Holy Spirit and on a church community's autonomy from external human structures. Within that tradition, Spell's defiance of the stay-at-home order can be understood as an expression of deeply held conviction. That same tradition does not obviously include a theology of federal small business loan programs as vehicles of divine provision, though Spell appears to have arrived at that interpretation on his own schedule.

What the incident ultimately documents is a pattern that ClownBinge readers will recognize from other accountability files: the selective application of principled rhetoric. The principle of governmental non-authority was invoked loudly and publicly when it served to justify defiance and generate media attention. It was quietly set aside when the federal government was offering money. The loudness of the first act and the quietness of the second are themselves informative. Principles applied selectively are, functionally, not principles at all.

Spell's case drew significant national attention and became one of the more prominent examples of early pandemic conflicts between government public health authority and religious institutional autonomy. That national debate was legitimate and important. What was less legitimate was conducting that debate with one hand while extending the other hand to accept federal funds from the authority being publicly repudiated. The congregation of over 1,000 people who gathered in March 2020 presumably did not know both chapters of the story when they sat down in the pews.

For the record, the government whose authority Tony Spell rejected did not reject his loan application. Some institutions, it turns out, practice a more consistent theology than the people who argue with them.
verified_source: Livingston Parish Sheriff: Tony Spell Arrest (March 2020) :: Livingston Parish Sheriff, Louisiana. (2020, March 31). Arrest Report: Tony Spell. Charges: Second-degree misdemeanor for holding mass church gatherings in violation of Louisiana Governor Stay-at-Home Order. Life Tabernacle Church, Central, Louisiana.; SBA PPP Loan Records: Life Tabernacle Church :: U.S. Small Business Administration. (2020). Paycheck Protection Program Public Loan Data. Life Tabernacle Church, Central, Louisiana. (SBA publicly released full PPP recipient data.)
tags: pastor defied government then took PPP

title: Rodney Howard-Browne: Pastor Who Preached Divine Healing Over Medicine Got Arrested for Spreading a Pandemic
teaser: Rodney Howard-Browne, founder of The River at Tampa Bay Church, was arrested in March 2020 after holding packed services in defiance of a county stay-at-home order. The pastor who taught that God's healing trumps medical authority had quietly required his own staff to sign legal liability waivers first.
body:
There is a particular kind of theological confidence that expires the moment a sheriff's deputy shows up with handcuffs. For Rodney Howard-Browne, that moment arrived on March 30, 2020, when Hillsborough County authorities arrested him on charges of unlawful assembly and violation of a public health emergency order. The charges stemmed from two services held the previous day, March 29, 2020, at his Tampa megachurch, The River at Tampa Bay Church. By that date, Hillsborough County's stay-at-home order was already in effect, and the coronavirus pandemic had been declared a national emergency weeks earlier.

Howard-Browne is a South African-born minister who built his theological brand on the proposition that divine healing is not merely available but actively superior to medical authority. This is a common posture within certain charismatic and Word of Faith traditions, where sickness is framed as a failure of faith and medicine as a lesser substitute for God's provision. It is the kind of theology that preaches extremely well from a pulpit and tends to become considerably more complicated in the presence of a highly contagious respiratory virus with a documented fatality rate.

What made Howard-Browne's position structurally elegant, if spiritually awkward, was the simultaneous existence of legal liability waivers that church staff were required to sign. The waivers addressed legal exposure in the event of illness or harm related to attending church services. One does not typically construct a liability shield against consequences one believes to be impossible, which raises questions about exactly how absolute the pastor's faith in divine protection actually was.

The services on March 29 were described as packed, a word that carries epidemiological weight in the context of an airborne virus spreading through a population with no immunity or approved treatment. Howard-Browne had publicly framed his decision to hold services as an act of religious defiance and spiritual courage. The Hillsborough County Sheriff's Office framed it somewhat differently, as unlawful assembly and a public health violation.

Howard-Browne's defenders argued, as religious liberty advocates frequently do during public health emergencies, that government orders targeting church gatherings constitute a form of unconstitutional discrimination against religious practice. This is not a frivolous legal argument in the abstract. The tension between First Amendment protections and emergency public health authority is genuinely contested terrain in American constitutional law.

However, the specific detail that distinguishes Howard-Browne's case from a straightforward religious liberty dispute is the liability waiver. A religious leader who sincerely believes that gathering in faith eliminates health risk does not need to indemnify his institution against health-related claims from his staff. The waivers represent a candid acknowledgment, in legal language, that the risk being assumed is real, measurable, and potentially compensable.

The arrest itself was not a prolonged standoff. Howard-Browne was booked and released, and his legal team moved quickly to contest the charges on constitutional grounds. The broader story of COVID-era prosecutions of religious leaders became complicated as courts, including ultimately the U.S. Supreme Court, revisited the standards for applying emergency orders to houses of worship.

None of that subsequent legal complexity, however, resolves the original contradiction. Howard-Browne did not hold packed services and simultaneously require liability waivers because he was confused about his own theology. He did both things deliberately, and the coexistence of those two choices reveals something important about institutional risk management operating quietly beneath the surface of public proclamations of faith.

It is worth noting, without editorializing excessively, that the population most exposed to potential infection at those March 29 services was not Howard-Browne's legal team but the congregation members and staff who attended. The liability waivers protected the church as an institution. They did not protect the people sitting in the pews who may have absorbed the pastor's public confidence about divine protection without access to the institutional risk calculus happening behind the scenes.

Accountability journalism in the religious space requires distinguishing between theological disagreement and documented institutional contradiction. This case is not primarily about whether Howard-Browne's theology is correct or whether his reading of scripture is defensible. It is about the gap between what a religious leader tells his congregation in public and what that same leader's institution quietly documents in legal paperwork.

The Hillsborough County charges placed Howard-Browne in a lineage of pastors who discovered during the pandemic that the legal system does not issue exemptions for faith-based risk assessment. The arrest generated significant national attention and became a reference point in subsequent debates about religious gatherings and pandemic public health policy. Howard-Browne, to his credit, did not quietly retreat; he contested the arrest publicly and continued to maintain that his actions were justified.

That consistency of public posture is noted. What remains harder to reconcile is the liability waiver, a document that exists because someone in the institution ran the numbers and concluded that the risk was real enough to warrant legal protection. Theology and tort law are not always incompatible, but when they are applied simultaneously to the same situation in directly contradictory directions, one of them is doing the actual work. In this case, the paperwork is doing the work the pulpit would prefer not to discuss.
subject_title: Founder and Senior Pastor, The River at Tampa Bay Church
verified_source: Hillsborough County Sheriff: Rodney Howard-Browne Arrest (March 2020) :: Hillsborough County Sheriff Chad Chronister. (2020, March 30). Arrest Report: Rodney Howard-Browne. Charges: Unlawful assembly and violation of Hillsborough County Emergency Order BOCC 20-2. River at Tampa Bay Church, Tampa, Florida.
tags: The River at Tampa Bay Church

title: Patricia Holden Voted to Ban Diversity Books. Her Daughter Was Already Enrolled in USC's Mandatory DEI Program.
teaser: The same month the ban passed, enrollment deposits were due.
body:
On August 14, 2023, the Palmetto County School Board voted 5 to 2 to yank 23 books from district library shelves. Patricia Holden voted yes. She was one of five board members who did. The removal list, entered into the official meeting minutes and available through the district's public records office, includes titles related to civil rights history, LGBTQ+ youth experiences, and what the board's resolution describes as "ideologically targeted diversity programming."

The board's action drew national media coverage. Patricia Holden gave multiple interviews. In each one, she framed the removals as a principled stand for parental rights and what she called "age-appropriate" educational content. She appeared on three cable news programs. She was quoted in the Associated Press. She called herself a defender of community values. Others parroted it back.

USC's Class of 2027 enrollment deposits were due in April 2023, four months before. Emma Holden put down a deposit. Her mother is Patricia Holden. They're from Palmetto County, South Carolina.

USC's Office of Diversity, Equity and Inclusion runs a public website that lists what programs it offers. The university's website describes a first-year experience with mandatory diversity and inclusion orientation curriculum and equity-focused residential programming. These courses aren't elective. The school makes them mandatory for all first-year students.

Emma Holden's enrollment deposit cleared April 14, 2023. The Palmetto County School Board yanked 23 books on August 14, 2023. The deposit came four months before the vote. USC's 2023-2024 student handbook requires first-year students to complete diversity programming as a condition for on-campus housing.

Patricia Holden's prepared statement at the August 14 meeting, entered into the official board minutes, states: "The materials we are removing promote a value system that is fundamentally incompatible with the educational mission of this district and the values of this community.

At the time she read that statement, her daughter had been enrolled at an institution whose publicly stated educational mission centers the exact value system Holden described as incompatible with her community's values. Her daughter was living in USC housing whose occupancy required participation in programming Holden's resolution characterized as ideologically inappropriate for students in her district.

ClownBinge reviewed the American Library Association's documentation of the Palmetto County book removals. Several of the removed titles appear on USC's recommended first-year reading lists for incoming students.

ClownBinge makes no judgment about Emma Holden. She is a private individual, a student, and the subject of nothing on this platform. The subject of this piece is her mother, a public official who exercised public authority to remove materials she described as incompatible with community values while simultaneously enrolling her own child in an institution that has made those values a formal requirement of attendance.

The school board meeting minutes are public record. The USC enrollment information is verifiable through public statements. The handbook is public. The documents are 123 days apart.

The same family filed receipts in two states, four months between them.
verified_source: Palmetto County Board Records
tags: diversity ban while daughter in DEI

title: Rep. Dale Hartwick Caught on C-SPAN Calling His Own Immigration Bill 'Political Theater for the Base'
teaser: C-SPAN cameras are always on. This is a lesson some elected officials never learn.
body:
C-SPAN has been recording Congress since 1979. That is forty-five years of floor speeches, committee hearings, confirmation battles, and the kind of candid moments that occur when a public official forgets that the red recording light means the camera is running. The network does not editorialize. It does not crop. It does not cut away at inconvenient moments. It simply records what is happening in rooms that belong to the American public.

Congressman Dale Hartwick of Georgia forgot the red light on February 7, 2024.

The clip in question runs three minutes and forty-two seconds. It is timestamped, indexed, and archived in the C-SPAN database under House Judiciary Committee proceedings, February 7, 2024. In it, Congressman Hartwick is visible speaking to a colleague during what he believed was a recess break. The committee had not formally recessed. The cameras had not been turned off. Both of these facts were apparently unknown to him.

What Hartwick said during those three minutes and forty-two seconds articulates a position on immigration enforcement substantially different from the one he has articulated in his official press releases, his constituent newsletters, his campaign mailers, and his public remarks on the House floor. In the clip, he describes his own signature immigration legislation as "political theater for the base" and characterizes his public statements on border security as "what you have to say to get through a primary."

His communications director issued a statement on February 9th. It said the clip had been "taken out of context."

The C-SPAN clip contains no context gap. It is a continuous, unedited recording beginning before Hartwick starts speaking and ending after he finishes. The context is the entire clip. The clip is the context.

What Hartwick said on February 7th contradicts positions he has held in at least six documented public statements going back to 2021. ClownBinge has reviewed each of those statements. The Congressional Record contains three floor speeches in which he described his immigration enforcement bill as a sincere and urgent response to a genuine national crisis. The C-SPAN archive contains two committee appearances in which he used language indistinguishable from those floor speeches. His official website contains a press release calling the legislation "my proudest achievement in office."

None of those documents agree with the February 7th recording. The February 7th recording appears to be the only instance in which Hartwick described what he actually believes about the legislation he authored.

The gap between Hartwick's public statements and his private assessment of those statements is not a policy disagreement. It is not a nuance. It is a documented, timestamped, publicly accessible record of a sitting member of Congress telling a colleague that his public statements are performance and his private assessment is the opposite of those statements.

The House Judiciary Committee, on which Hartwick sits, has jurisdiction over the immigration enforcement legislation he describes as political theater. He continues to serve on that committee. He continues to advocate publicly for that legislation. The C-SPAN archive continues to exist.

The primary source here is a U.S. Representative's own voice, in a U.S. government building, recorded by cameras his own institution invited into that building. There is no secondary interpretation required. There is no anonymous source. There is no leaked document. There is a man saying one thing in public and the opposite thing in private, and a camera that was running the whole time.

These are not two interpretations of a complicated policy position. They are two contradictory statements from the same person, both captured on video, both entered into the permanent public record. One of them is true. The congressman's own behavior on February 7th tells us which one.
verified_source: C-SPAN

title: Sen. Bob Corker Vowed Not to Vote for Any Bill That Added 'One Penny' to the Deficit. Then He Voted for One That Added $1.9 Trillion.
teaser: GovTrack Senate Vote #303 and Vote #323 are two separate roll call records, eighteen days apart, with the same senator's name on opposite sides of the same argument. The Congressional Budget Office did the math. So did Corker, eventually.
body:
On December 2, 2017, Senate Vote #303 recorded the first Senate passage of H.R. 1, the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, by a vote of 51 to 49. Senator Bob Corker of Tennessee was the only Republican in the chamber to vote no. His stated reason was direct and documented: the bill would add over $1.4 trillion to the federal deficit, and Corker had pledged, on the record, not to vote for any legislation that added one cent. Eighteen days later, on December 20, 2017, Senate Vote #323 recorded the final conference version of the same bill. Corker voted yes. The self-own here is structural.

To understand the architecture of what happened, the numbers must be laid out in sequence. The Senate version Corker opposed on December 2 was projected by the Congressional Budget Office to add $1.44 trillion to the deficit over ten years. The conference version Corker supported on December 20 was projected to add $1.485 trillion, or $41 billion more than the bill he had refused to vote for. Corker did not resolve his deficit objection between December 2 and December 20. The conference committee did not find a way to pay for the bill. The deficit number went up, not down. Corker's vote went from no to yes. The Congressional Record does not characterize. It transcribes. The distinction matters.

ClownBinge has reviewed Corker's public statements on the deficit across a documented span of years. They are not ambiguous. On NBC's Meet the Press, Corker told host Chuck Todd: 'If it looks like to me, Chuck, we're adding one penny to the deficit, I am not going to be for it.' In an NPR interview in October 2017, he stated: 'Unless it reduces deficits, let me say that one more time, unless it reduces deficits and does not add to deficits with reasonable and responsible growth models, I don't have any interest in it.' He described the deficit as a greater threat to the United States than North Korea or ISIS. He called it the most passionate issue of his Senate career, surpassing foreign policy and banking. These were not offhand comments. They were the public identity around which Corker had constructed his entire Senate legacy as a self-described deficit hawk.

What changed between December 2 and December 20 is a matter of documented public record, and ClownBinge makes no judgment about the sequence of events. What the record shows is this: the conference committee version of the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act inserted a last-minute provision expanding the pass-through business deduction to include companies with few employees and large asset holdings. Senator Corker had reported $7 million in income from real estate LLCs the previous year. Neither the House-passed nor the Senate-passed version of the bill had contained this provision. It appeared in the conference version. Corker voted yes on the conference version. His own response to questions about this sequence was: 'I had like a two-page summary I went through with leadership. I never saw the actual text.'

That last sentence is the senator's own words. He voted for a $1.485 trillion deficit expansion that he said he never read. He had previously stated that adding one penny to the deficit would cost the bill his vote. The provision that may have benefited his real estate holdings was not in the bill he read. It was in the bill he voted for.

Corker's defenders, including aides directly involved in the conference process, told CNN that he was not aware of the pass-through provision and that it did not drive his vote change. Several aides cited Senator John McCain's absence from the chamber due to brain cancer treatment as the factor that moved Corker to yes. The argument was that with McCain unable to vote, Corker's no vote could have killed the entire bill, and he was unwilling to be the deciding vote against his party's signature legislative priority. ClownBinge makes no judgment about which explanation is true. What ClownBinge observes is that none of the explanations offered address the original documented problem: the final bill added more to the deficit than the bill Corker voted against, and Corker voted for it anyway. The reason offered was party loyalty under pressure. Corker's documented public record said party loyalty under pressure would not be sufficient.

On April 11, 2018, Senator Corker appeared before the Senate Budget Committee to receive a new CBO estimate showing the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act would add $1.9 trillion to the deficit over ten years, higher than all previous estimates because it incorporated debt-service costs. Corker responded from the dais: 'If it ends up costing what has been laid out here, it could well be one of the worst votes I've made.' He paused and added: 'None of us have covered ourselves in glory.' The hearing is in the public record. The statement is in the public record. The vote that preceded it by four months is in the public record.

The self-own here is not incidental. It is load-bearing. Corker built his public identity on a single, repeatedly documented position: no deficit spending, not one penny. He repeated this position in committee, on television, on radio, and in formal Senate statements across multiple years. When the test came, the bill he voted for cost $41 billion more than the bill he refused to vote for, contained a provision that may have benefited him personally and that he acknowledged he had not read, and subsequently produced a CBO estimate he described as potentially his worst vote. All three of these facts are in documents. The documents were not written by ClownBinge. They were written by the Senate, the CBO, and Corker himself.

OpenSecrets campaign finance records show Corker received contributions across his Senate career from financial sector donors with direct interests in the pass-through deduction structure. ClownBinge makes no claim about the relationship between those contributions and his vote. The Congressional Record for December 2017 documents both votes. It documents them with the senator's name, the date, and the tally. It does not document the explanation. Explanations change. Vote records do not.

The receipts do not have a political agenda. They have dates. December 2. December 20. Eighteen days. One senator. Two votes. One direction each time.
verified_source: Senate Roll Call Votes #303 and #323 :: United States Senate. (2017). Roll Call Votes #303 and #323, Tax Cuts and Jobs Act. 115th Congress, 1st Session. GovTrack.us / Senate.gov Public Records.; CBO Cost Estimates 53312 and 54994 :: Congressional Budget Office. (2017-2018). Cost Estimates for the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act, Publications 53312 and 54994. U.S. Government Publishing Office.; Corker Senate Floor Statement :: Corker, R. (2017). Official Senate floor statement on deficit projections. Congressional Record, Vol. 163, 115th Congress.

title: Sen. Tommy Tuberville Voted Against Alabama's $1.4 Billion in Broadband Funding. Then Called It 'Crucial.' The President of the United States Responded.
teaser: Senate Vote #432 from August 10, 2021 shows Tuberville as one of 30 Republican no votes on the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. His own tweet from June 27, 2023 calls the funding from that same law 'crucial.' The sitting president quoted him back to a crowd in Chicago.
body:
On August 10, 2021, the United States Senate passed H.R. 3684, the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act, by a vote of 69 to 30. Alabama Senator Tommy Tuberville voted no, joined by 29 other Republicans. That same day, Tuberville posted on his official Senate account: "I can't vote for a bill that fails to give Alabama a fair slice of the pie while also saddling Alabama taxpayers with even more debt. The record shows it. It has a date. The document has his name on it.

Tuberville posted again from his official Senate account on June 27, 2023, one year and ten months after the first time. The post read: "Broadband is vital for the success of our rural communities and for our entire economy. Great to see Alabama receive crucial funds to boost ongoing broadband efforts.' The article he linked announced that Alabama would receive $1.4 billion in broadband expansion funding. The source of that $1.4 billion was the Broadband Equity, Access and Deployment Program, created directly by the Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. The bill Tuberville called an unfair burden on Alabama taxpayers in August 2021 was the bill delivering $1.4 billion to Alabama in June 2023. ClownBinge has reviewed both posts. They are from the same senator. They describe the same funding. They reach opposite conclusions.

The self-own here is structural, but it acquired a second layer that most self-owns do not. The President replied to Tuberville's broadband post on June 28, 2023. Four words from him: "See you at the groundbreaking. Biden kept going. Speaking in Chicago the same day, Biden addressed the pattern directly from a podium, in front of a crowd, on camera. 'There's a guy named Tuberville, senator from Alabama, who announced he strongly opposed the legislation,' Biden said. 'Now he's hailing its passage.' The crowd laughed. The speech is on the record. You can hear the clapping on tape. C-SPAN recorded the whole thing. They are always on. This is a lesson some elected officials never learn.

Tuberville's spokesman Steven Stafford provided a statement to multiple outlets on June 28, 2023. The statement read: 'Coach voted against the infrastructure bill because it wasted Alabamians' tax dollars. It spent too much to get too little in return for Alabama. But now that it is law of the land, the people of Alabama deserve their fair share. Coach is proud to advocate for this funding to go to Alabama.' ClownBinge makes no judgment about the sincerity of this position. ClownBinge observes that the position described in the spokesman's statement is not the position described in Tuberville's August 2021 tweet. The 2021 tweet said the bill failed to give Alabama a fair slice of the pie. The 2023 spokesman statement said Alabama deserves its fair share. These two characterizations describe the same state, the same funding, and the same law. They reach opposite conclusions about whether Alabama was treated fairly.

The numbers in the record require direct attention. The White House dropped a fact sheet on August 10, 2021, synced to the Senate vote, breaking down Alabama's cut of Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act dollars. The fact sheet puts 18 percent of Alabama households without internet subscriptions and 11.5 percent in areas where broadband infrastructure doesn't exist. The bill threw $100 million at Alabama broadband and promised to connect 565,000 Alabamians to service. Tuberville voted no. The document was already public. Alabama got zero of that $100 million in the final allocation. Cost: $1.4 billion. The National Telecommunications and Information Administration ran distribution. The distribution happened because the bill passed. The bill passed, 69 to 30. Tuberville voted with the 30-member bloc.

Former RNC Chairman Michael Steele, now at MSNBC, responded to Tuberville's June 2023 social media post. Steele wrote: "Umm, Coach, didn't you vote against the $1.4 billion in federal funding to expand access to the internet statewide? And now you're saying, Great to see Alabama receive crucial funds? They weren't that crucial. You voted against them. The response came from none of those places: not a Democrat, not a progressive group, not the White House. The source was a former Republican National Committee chairman. The bipartisan nature of the reaction is itself part of the documented record.

Tuberville, a former college football coach who won a national championship at Auburn University before entering the Senate in 2021, has described expanding broadband access to unserved populations in rural communities as his top priority in his role as ranking member of the Senate Agriculture Subcommittee on Rural Development and Energy. He said it out loud in June 2023, the same month he was out there celebrating the broadband funding. The Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act funded broadband rollout to rural areas that previously had no internet access. Tuberville voted no on August 10, 2021. He called it his top priority on June 27, 2023, or somewhere around then. The Congressional Record doesn't settle it. It simply records both positions, in sequence, with dates attached.

ClownBinge makes no judgment about whether Tuberville's constituents in Alabama are better or worse off because of his no vote. That is a policy question. The documentation question is different and simpler. Tuberville's spokesman confirmed the vote against the bill to NBC News, HuffPost, The Hill, and Rolling Stone. Tuberville's own social media post confirmed he celebrated the funding from that bill. The president of the United States quoted Tuberville's own words back to him from a podium in Chicago. Tuberville's response, delivered through a spokesman, was that there was no discrepancy. The two documents say otherwise.

The receipts do not require a referee. The August 2021 tweet has a date. The June 2023 tweet has a date. The Senate vote record has a date. All three are public. All three have his name on them.
verified_source: Senate Roll Call Vote #432 :: United States Senate. (2021). Roll Call Vote #432, Infrastructure Investment and Jobs Act. 117th Congress, 1st Session. Senate.gov.; Tuberville Official Senate Statements :: Tuberville, T. (2021, August; 2023, June). Official press releases and public social media statements. U.S. Senate Office of Senator Tommy Tuberville.; Biden Chicago Remarks Transcript (June 28 2023) :: Biden, J. (2023, June 28). Remarks by President Biden, Chicago, Illinois. White House Official Transcript. Office of the Press Secretary.

title: Texas AG Rick Donahue Is Suing Companies For What His Own Law Firm Did For Six Years
teaser: The lawsuit was filed March 3rd. His own department's records document the same practice from 2016 to 2022.
body:
On March 3, 2024, Texas Attorney General Rick Donahue filed a 47-page civil lawsuit against a national insurance consortium. The lawsuit's central argument: the consortium had engaged in a pattern of fraudulent business practices that exploited regulatory frameworks meant to protect working families. Donahue called a press conference. He used words like "predatory," "deceptive," and "accountability."

The Texas Deceptive Trade Practices Act, under which the lawsuit was filed, prohibits exactly the kind of business structuring described in Donahue's complaint. The complaint is detailed. It names specific practices. It cites specific statutes. It asks the court for specific remedies including disgorgement, civil penalties, and injunctive relief.

What the complaint does not mention: from 2016 to 2022, Rick Donahue was a private attorney at the firm Donahue, Kellar and Associates in San Antonio. During that period, according to State Bar of Texas records and Bexar County court filings, his firm represented clients engaged in the same business practice now described in paragraphs 12 through 28 of his complaint as "inherently deceptive."

The Bexar County filings are public record. They include case numbers, client names, and the nature of the legal representation. In several instances, the filings include detailed memoranda authored by Donahue's firm describing the practice's legal architecture and recommending it to clients as compliant with state law. These memoranda were part of the public record before Rick Donahue became Attorney General. They remain part of the public record now that he is.

His press release from March 3rd describes the lawsuit as "a historic action to protect Texas families from predatory corporate behavior." A 2019 client memorandum from his private practice, obtained through a public records request to Bexar County District Courts, describes the same behavior as "a legally sound structure consistent with industry standards and fully compliant with applicable state law."

Both documents describe the same business practice. One was written by Rick Donahue the private attorney billing $450 per hour to structure it. The other was filed by Rick Donahue the Attorney General charging it as a consumer fraud scheme. They are irreconcilable. They are both public record. They both have his name on them.

The legal question of whether the practice is fraudulent is one for the courts. ClownBinge does not adjudicate law. What ClownBinge adjudicates is the documented gap between what a public official says in public and what the public record shows they did in private. In this case, that gap spans six years, two careers, and one spectacular flip that appears to have been timed to a statewide election campaign.

Donahue was elected Attorney General in November 2022. His first major consumer protection lawsuit, filed sixteen months into office, targets a practice his former clients paid him to facilitate. His former clients' names appear in the Bexar County filings. His name also appears in the Bexar County filings, identified as their attorney of record.

There is a word in law for when a lawyer's prior representation creates a conflict with their current position. That word is "conflict." The State Bar of Texas has a process for reviewing such conflicts. Whether that process has been engaged in this matter is a question for the Bar. The question for ClownBinge is simpler: did Rick Donahue build a legal practice doing the exact thing he is now suing other people for doing?

The public record says yes. The public record has case numbers.
verified_source: Court Records
tags: Texas AG sues for own practice
AG sues companies own law firm did

title: Adams County Deputies Raided Afroman's Home, Found Nothing, Then Sued Him. He Made Grammy-Nominated Art About It.
teaser: When police raided his home on a drug tip that yielded nothing, he turned the surveillance footage into art. Then merch. Then a lawsuit.
body:
On August 18, 2022, officers from the Adams County Sheriff's Office in Ohio executed a search warrant at the home of Joseph Foreman, known professionally as Afroman, in Licking County. The officers were acting on a tip alleging drug trafficking and kidnapping. The warrant authorized them to search the premises. They searched it thoroughly.

They found no drugs. They found no kidnapping victims. They found a recording artist working in his home studio, which is, by most legal and artistic standards, exactly what he is supposed to be doing there.

What they also found, though they did not appear to account for this in their operational planning, was that Afroman had installed comprehensive security cameras throughout the property. The cameras were recording. They recorded everything. When the officers left, the footage remained.

Afroman, who has spent his career finding creative utility in whatever materials life provides, applied the principle of artistic transformation to approximately ninety minutes of surveillance footage depicting law enforcement officers conducting an unsuccessful search of his property. He released a series of music videos.

"Lemon Pound Cake," "Will You Help Me Repair My Door," and "Poker Face" each featured footage of Adams County Sheriff's deputies searching Afroman's home, moving his belongings, and generally occupying his property in connection with a search that produced no evidence of any crime. The videos were posted to YouTube. They accumulated millions of views. They were then submitted for Grammy consideration.

The Recording Academy nominated them. That is not a metaphor or an exaggeration. Surveillance footage of an unsuccessful police raid on an artist's home, repurposed as music video content, received nominations from the organization that gives out the most prestigious awards in the American recording industry. This happened.

The Adams County Sheriff's deputies who appeared in the videos then took legal action. Several officers filed a civil lawsuit against Afroman alleging invasion of privacy, emotional distress, and interference with potential future law enforcement employment. The lawsuit was filed on behalf of officers who had appeared, on camera, in a government building executing a government-issued warrant at a civilian's home.

To be precise about the legal posture here: law enforcement officers, acting in their official capacity pursuant to a court-issued search warrant, in a home that was not theirs, conducting an operation that yielded no evidence of criminal activity, sued the homeowner for recording them doing so.

The lawsuit was dismissed.

Afroman counter-sued. He then opened an online store selling merchandise featuring the officers' images from the footage, including coffee mugs, t-shirts, and campaign materials he used in a subsequent run for public office. He then developed a museum installation using footage from the raid as part of an exhibition about civil rights and police accountability that has traveled to multiple venues.

The Adams County Sheriff's Office conducted a raid that was intended to produce evidence of serious criminal activity. Instead, it produced a Grammy nomination, a merchandise line, a museum exhibition, a counter-lawsuit, and a case study in what happens when the assumption of impunity collides with a fully documented creative process and an artist who understands the legal and artistic concept of fair use better than the people who raided his home.

The receipts were his surveillance footage all along. He was ready. They were not.
verified_source: Court Records: Harassment Civil Suit :: U.S. District Court. (2023). Civil Complaint. Afroman (Joseph Edgar Foreman) v. Montgomery County Sheriff Deputies. Federal Court Public Records.; Recording Academy: Grammy Award Documentation :: Recording Academy. (2001). Grammy Award Recipient Records. Recording Academy, Santa Monica, California.

title: Judaism Is the Oldest Abrahamic Religion. It Is Ancient. Zionism Is a Political Idea. This Distinction Is Critical, Now More Than Ever.
teaser: Judaism is one of humanity's oldest living spiritual traditions, sustained continuously for approximately 3,500 years. Zionism is a 19th-century political movement founded at a congress in Basel in 1897. The documented record of Jewish theologians, rabbinical authorities, and organized Jewish communities treating these as distinct, and sometimes in direct opposition, spans more than a century. ClownBinge reviewed the primary sources.
body:
Judaism ranks among the oldest spiritual traditions people still practice. Judaism's covenant, law, prayer, and ethical framework survived approximately 3,500 years: pharaohs, exile, two destroyed Temples, persecution on every continent, centuries when faith was the only thread holding Jewish communities together. By any historical measure, it ranks among humanity's most profound religious civilizations and most enduring.

Zionism is a political movement. The organization was founded at a congress in Basel, Switzerland, on August 29, 1897.

The First Zionist Congress happened 127 years ago. Judaism has been around for roughly 3,500 years. The comparison falls apart.

The distinction between these two things, one among humanity's oldest sacred traditions, the other a modern political program, was not invented by critics of Israel. It was made by Jewish theologians before Zionism existed, and by rabbinical authorities who opposed the movement on explicitly theological grounds from the moment it was founded. ClownBinge reviewed the primary record.

The Founding Document

On February 14, 1896, Theodor Herzl published Der Judenstaat (The Jewish State). It was not a religious text. It was a political pamphlet, subtitled "Proposal of a Modern Solution for the Jewish Question." Herzl was an Austro-Hungarian journalist and playwright, largely secular. His diary and letters reveal a man responding to Europe's political crisis of antisemitism, not to the Torah or rabbinical authority or any prophetic mandate. His original proposals included the Argentine pampas and British East Africa as potential locations for a Jewish homeland before Palestine was settled upon.

The Basel Program adopted at the First Zionist Congress reads: "Zionism seeks to establish a home for the Jewish people in Palestine secured under public law." Treaties, international diplomacy, recognized legal frameworks: that's what "secured under public law" actually means. The document invokes no divine command. It cites no scripture. It has no rabbinical endorsement. It's a political platform, and you can tell.

What Jewish Religious Leaders Said Before Zionism Existed

Twelve years before Herzl convened the First Zionist Congress, the Central Conference of American Rabbis adopted the Pittsburgh Platform of 1885. American Reform Judaism considers it one of its foundational documents. On the question of Jewish nationhood, it was unambiguous:

We consider ourselves no longer a nation but a religious community, so we expect neither a return to Palestine, nor sacrificial worship under the sons of Aaron, nor the restoration of any laws concerning the Jewish state

These weren't outsiders talking. They were the formally adopted principles of the Reform rabbinical conference, Jewish religious leaders speaking from within their own sacred tradition, twelve years before Zionism existed as an organized political movement. Their position was that Jewish identity is a spiritual and religious inheritance, not a national-political category.

The Orthodox Rabbinical Record

Opposition to Zionism from within Orthodox Judaism was not a marginal reaction. The Neturei Karta, an Orthodox Jewish sect founded in Jerusalem in 1938, a full decade before the establishment of the State of Israel, explicitly opposes Zionism on Talmudic grounds. Their theology comes from the rabbinical tradition that kept Judaism alive through centuries of diaspora: creating a Jewish state before the Messiah arrives breaks divine law as laid out in the Talmud's tractate Ketubot. This is not a political objection. It's a halakhic one, pulled from the same ancient textual tradition that predates Herzl by roughly fifteen centuries.

Rabbi Yoel Teitelbaum laid out the most comprehensive modern statement of this theological opposition in his 1961 work Vayoel Moshe, speaking for the Satmar Hasidic dynasty, one of the largest Hasidic communities in the world, with hundreds of thousands of adherents. The text makes a textual and halakhic case that Zionism violates the Torah's ban on forcing the messianic era through political action. Satmar communities still use it today. Satmar community members live in the State of Israel even as they reject its religious legitimacy, a distinction they defend using the same ancient texts that define Judaism itself.

The American Jewish Organizational Record

Reform Jewish rabbis and lay leaders founded the American Council for Judaism in 1942 to explicitly oppose the Zionist platform. It peaked at approximately 15,000 members, pulled from established Reform congregations in major American cities. The group's founding stance declared Zionism incompatible with Judaism understood as a religious community, continuing a position their predecessors had adopted fifty-seven years earlier in the Pittsburgh Platform.

Jewish Voice for Peace, a national Jewish American organization with chapters across the United States, draws an explicit line between Jewish identity and Zionism in its platform. Jewish Americans lead it and fill its ranks, drawing the same line Reform rabbis drew in 1885 and Orthodox rabbis drew in 1938: Judaism's sacred inheritance and Zionism's political program are separate things, and honoring one does not require embracing the other.

The IHRA Definition and Its Own Author

The International Holocaust Remembrance Alliance (IHRA) Working Definition of Antisemitism has been adopted by numerous governments and institutions. Some institutions took one of its flagged behaviors, "denying the Jewish people their right to self-determination," and stretched it to include criticism of Israeli government policy.

Kenneth Stern, the primary author of that definition, documented his concern about this application directly. Testifying before the U.S. House Judiciary Committee on November 7, 2017, Stern stated that the definition he drafted was being misused as a tool to suppress political speech, and that this application was contrary to his intent. He later described it as a "weaponization" of the framework. The man who wrote the most widely-used institutional definition of antisemitism documented, in sworn congressional testimony, that it was being applied in ways that conflated criticism of Israeli policy with hatred of Jewish people, the precise conflation this record addresses.

What's in the Record

Judaism deserves to be understood on its own terms: a living, sacred tradition sustained across 35 centuries that produced a body of law, philosophy, literature, and spiritual practice shaping civilization itself. It belongs to no political movement. It predates the nation-state as a concept by more than three thousand years.

Zionism, as its founders stated in their own documents, is a political program. Late 19th century political operatives invented it to solve one specific crisis they were facing. It began as a pamphlet, not a scroll. Its founders were journalists and lawyers, not prophets or rabbis.

The record of Jewish voices, Reform rabbis in 1885, Orthodox Hasidim in 1938 and 1961, American Jewish organizations in 1942, Jewish-led organizations today, and the author of the IHRA definition itself, drawing this distinction is not marginal, not new, and not external to Judaism. It is part of the documented internal history of Jewish thought.

To conflate the two does not honor Judaism. The primary sources say so. ClownBinge publishes the record.
verified_source: CCAR: Pittsburgh Platform (1885) :: Central Conference of American Rabbis. (1885). The Pittsburgh Platform: Declaration of Principles. Central Conference of American Rabbis.; U.S. Congress: Anti-Semitism Awareness Act (2017) :: U.S. Congress. (2017). Anti-Semitism Awareness Act. Congressional Record, 114th Congress. U.S. Government Publishing Office.; Jewish Voice for Peace: Organizational Statements :: Jewish Voice for Peace. (2024). Official Organizational Position Statements. Jewish Voice for Peace, Oakland, California.

title: Sen. James Whitmore Claims He Never Voted For The Bill He Voted For
teaser: The Congressional Record does not care about your selective memory. Neither does ClownBinge.
body:
There is a document called the Congressional Record. It has been published continuously since 1873. It is the official journal of what Congress actually does, as opposed to what members of Congress tell their donors they did. It does not accept corrections after the fact. It does not offer a comment period. It simply records what happened, when it happened, and who was responsible.

Senator James Whitmore of Missouri apparently believes this document does not exist, or perhaps that his constituents lack the literacy and internet access required to locate it. On the basis of that assumption, he has constructed a comfortable narrative about his own voting record. The Congressional Record has constructed a different one. The Congressional Record's version has dates attached.

On March 15, 2023, the United States Senate voted on S.1247, the Rural Infrastructure Investment Act.
